announcement.setting: An announcement plays in the music building.
announcement.text: Attention, music students. The practice rooms in the basement are being renovated over spring break, so they'll be closed from March 10th through the 17th. During that week, please use the rooms on the second floor instead, which are available by reservation only. The grand piano in Room 8 has just been tuned and is reserved for graduate recitals. Remember to log out of the booking system when you finish, so others can use the space. Thank you.

questions.q: When will the basement practice rooms be closed?

All of spring

questions.q: Who is the newly tuned grand piano reserved for?

Faculty
Visitors
Graduate recitals

announcement.setting: A dining services announcement plays in the cafeteria.
announcement.text: Hi, everyone. A quick reminder about meal plans for next semester. The deadline to change your plan is July 25th, and no changes will be accepted after that date. The new 'flex' plan adds twenty guest passes you can share with friends. Late-night dining at the grill will now run until 1 a.m. on weekends. Also, the vegetarian station is expanding to both dining halls starting in the fall. Visit the dining office to update your plan.

questions.q: What is the deadline to change a meal plan?
options: July 25th
August 1st
The first day of fall
There is no deadline
questions.why: 更改餐飲方案的截止日是七月二十五日。

questions.q: How many guest passes does the new flex plan add?

Fifteen
Twenty
Thirty

announcement.setting: An announcement plays at the campus bike-share station.

Three stations will be temporarily closed on Wednesday for a software update. Remember that helmets are available to borrow at the recreation desk. Ride safely and lock your bike properly.

announcement.setting: A weather-related transit announcement plays across campus.

Only the main loop will operate, every thirty minutes, from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. The outer campus routes are suspended for the day. If classes are canceled, an alert will be sent to your phone by 6 a.m. Please allow extra travel time and wear appropriate footwear. Sidewalks will be salted, but use caution on the steps. Stay warm.

Every hour

By 7 p.m.

If you won't be home, leave your door unlocked or sign the access form at the front desk by Wednesday night. Warmth returns by afternoon.

talk.text: Okay, let's pick up where we left off, with one of the great mysteries of the animal world: how migrating birds find their way across thousands of miles. First, consider the Arctic tern. Every year, this small seabird flies from the Arctic all the way to Antarctica and back, a round trip of nearly forty thousand miles. Now, what's interesting is that young terns can make this journey even when they've never done it before. So how do they know where to go? Well, researchers have found that birds rely on several different tools at once. During the day, they use the position of the sun. At night, they read the patterns of the stars. But here's the really surprising part: birds can also sense the Earth's magnetic field. Tiny particles in their beaks act almost like a built-in compass, telling them which direction is north. Think of it as having several backup maps. If clouds hide the stars, the magnetic sense simply takes over. This layering of systems is exactly why migration is so reliable, and why these birds so rarely get lost.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why the Arctic tern is an endangered species.
How migrating birds find their way over long distances.
How birds build their nests in the Arctic.
Why some birds fail to complete migration.
questions.why: 全文主旨是候鳥如何在長途遷徙中辨識方向。

questions.q: According to the professor, how far does the Arctic tern travel round trip?
options: About four thousand miles.
About ten thousand miles.
About forty thousand miles.
About one hundred thousand miles.
questions.why: 教授說北極燕鷗來回一趟將近四萬英里。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a bird flying on a cloudy night?
options: It will most likely become lost.
It will stop flying until morning.
It will simply follow other birds.
It can still navigate using its magnetic sense.
questions.why: 星星被雲遮住時,鳥仍能靠磁場感應繼續導航。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the young terns?
options: To show that navigation does not depend on experience.
To explain why young birds often die in migration.
To compare terns with other kinds of seabirds.
To describe how adult terns teach their young.
questions.why: 提到幼鳥是為了說明導航能力不需靠經驗學習。

talk.text: Today I want to talk about a single invention that reshaped the world: the printing press. Now, before the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, usually by monks. As you can imagine, this was incredibly slow. A single Bible might take a scribe more than a year to complete, and the finished book was so expensive that only the wealthy or the church could own one. Then, around 1440, a German craftsman named Johannes Gutenberg developed a press that used movable metal type. Here's why that mattered: instead of carving a whole page at once, you could arrange individual letters, print hundreds of copies, and then rearrange those same letters for the next page. Suddenly, a book that once took a year could be produced in a matter of days. What's really interesting is the ripple effect. As books became cheaper, more people learned to read. Ideas spread faster than ever before, crossing borders in weeks rather than decades. Many historians argue that without the printing press, later movements like the scientific revolution simply could not have happened so quickly.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the lecture?
options: The daily life of medieval monks.
How the printing press changed the world.
The history of the Bible in Europe.
The biography of Johannes Gutenberg.
questions.why: 主旨是印刷術如何改變了世界。

questions.q: Before the printing press, how were books in Europe produced?
options: They were copied by hand.
They were printed with wooden blocks.
They were imported from Asia.
They were dictated aloud to crowds.
questions.why: 印刷術之前,歐洲的書多由僧侶手抄。

questions.q: What does the professor suggest about the scientific revolution?
options: It was actually delayed by printing.
It had no connection to printing at all.
It happened faster because of the printing press.
It replaced the need for printed books.
questions.why: 教授暗示印刷術讓科學革命等運動得以更快發生。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that a Bible could take over a year to copy?
options: To praise the great skill of medieval scribes.
To explain why monks disliked their daily work.
To argue that old handmade books were better.
To show how slow and costly book-making once was.
questions.why: 舉聖經抄寫要一年多,是為了凸顯過去製書的緩慢與昂貴。

talk.text: Let's turn now to a classic finding in social psychology: the bystander effect. The idea is a little unsettling. It says that the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to step in and help. Now, you might expect the opposite, right? More people should mean more help. But research shows the reverse. Why does this happen? Psychologists point to something called diffusion of responsibility. When you're the only witness, you feel that helping is entirely up to you. But in a crowd, that responsibility gets spread out. Everyone assumes someone else will act, and so, often, no one does. There's also a second factor: people look to others for cues. If everyone around you seems calm, you may decide the situation isn't really an emergency after all. Now, what's encouraging is that simply knowing about this effect can change your behavior. Studies show that people who've learned about the bystander effect are more likely to help. So if you ever need assistance in a crowd, the advice is clear: don't just shout for help in general. Point to one specific person and ask them directly.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: Why large crowds form during emergencies.
How to become a professional first responder.
Why people simply enjoy watching others.
Why people in groups often fail to help in emergencies.
questions.why: 主旨是旁觀者效應,即人在群體中較不會出手相助。

questions.q: What is diffusion of responsibility?
options: The belief that helping is someone else's job.
The fear of being blamed for a mistake.
The tendency to panic inside a crowd.
The habit of copying calm people nearby.
questions.why: 責任分散指人在群體中認為救助是別人的責任。

questions.q: Why does the professor advise pointing to one specific person?
options: Because that person is usually a doctor.
To make the whole crowd feel calmer.
To place the responsibility on that individual.
Because shouting is considered quite rude.
questions.why: 指定特定的人,是為了把責任落在那個人身上,避免責任分散。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that people look to others for cues?
options: To give a second reason for the bystander effect.
To prove that crowds are usually very friendly.
To describe how most emergencies first begin.
To explain why people enjoy being in crowds.
questions.why: 提到人會看別人反應,是要補充旁觀者效應的第二個成因。

talk.text: Have you ever wondered why stars seem to twinkle, but planets usually don't? It's a great question, and the answer has almost nothing to do with the stars themselves. First, let's remember one key fact: stars are enormously far away. Even the closest ones are so distant that they appear as single, tiny points of light. Now, here's what happens. As that thin beam of starlight travels down through Earth's atmosphere, it passes through layers of air at different temperatures, all moving and swirling. Each layer bends the light slightly, and because the air keeps shifting, the light gets bent this way and that. To our eyes, that rapid bending looks like twinkling. So why don't planets twinkle the same way? Well, planets are much closer to us, so instead of a single point, they appear as a tiny disk. Think of it as many points of light bunched together. The atmosphere still bends each point, but the flickers average out, and the planet shines steadily. This is also why telescopes placed in space produce such sharp images: above the atmosphere, there's nothing left to make the light dance.

questions.q: What does the talk mainly explain?
options: Why the stars are so far from Earth.
Why stars twinkle but planets usually do not.
How large telescopes are built in space.
How planets manage to produce their own light.
questions.why: 主旨在解釋為何星星閃爍而行星通常不閃。

questions.q: According to the professor, what causes starlight to twinkle?
options: Sudden changes in the star's brightness.
The enormous physical size of the stars.
Moving layers of air in the atmosphere.
The steady rotation of the Earth.
questions.why: 星光閃爍是因為大氣中不斷流動的空氣層使光線偏折。

questions.q: Why do telescopes in space produce sharper images?
options: They are much closer to the stars.
They are used only to observe planets.
They rely on much larger mirrors.
There is no atmosphere to bend the light.
questions.why: 太空望遠鏡在大氣層之上,沒有空氣使光線偏折,所以影像更清晰。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that planets appear as a tiny disk?
options: To show that planets are larger than stars.
To explain why planets do not twinkle much.
To prove planets are closer than the moon.
To describe the different colors of planets.
questions.why: 提到行星呈小圓盤,是為了解釋為何它們的閃爍會被平均掉。

talk.text: Alright, let's start with one of the most fundamental ideas in economics: opportunity cost. Now, the term sounds technical, but the concept is something you deal with every single day. Simply put, the opportunity cost of any choice is the value of the next best thing you gave up to make it. Let me give you an example. Imagine you have one free evening. You could spend it working a shift that pays sixty dollars, or you could go to a concert. If you choose the concert, the opportunity cost isn't just the price of the ticket. It also includes the sixty dollars you didn't earn. So the real cost of that concert is higher than it first appears. Here's why this matters. Good decision-making, whether for a person or a whole country, means weighing not just what you gain, but what you give up. A government that spends heavily on one program has less to spend elsewhere. Economists like to say there's no such thing as a free lunch. Every choice, no matter how small, closes the door on something else, and recognizing that is the first step toward thinking like an economist.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the lecture?
options: The concept of opportunity cost.
How to save money on entertainment.
Why concert tickets are so expensive.
How governments raise their taxes.
questions.why: 主旨是介紹經濟學中的機會成本概念。

questions.q: In the example, what is the opportunity cost of going to the concert?
options: Only the price of the ticket.
The cost of getting transportation.
The ticket price plus the wages not earned.
Nothing, because the evening was free.
questions.why: 去演唱會的機會成本包含門票錢加上沒賺到的六十元工資。

questions.q: What does the professor imply about a government's spending?
options: It should always avoid very large programs.
Spending on one thing leaves less for others.
Governments never face any opportunity costs.
Free programs truly cost nothing in the end.
questions.why: 教授暗示政府把錢花在某項計畫,就少了錢用在別處。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that there is no free lunch?
options: To recommend cooking more meals at home.
To criticize overpriced local restaurants.
To introduce a new and unrelated topic.
To sum up the idea that every choice has a cost.
questions.why: 引用「天下沒有白吃的午餐」是為了總結每個選擇都有代價的概念。

talk.text: Okay, so today I want to talk about how honeybees communicate. Now, when a forager bee finds a good patch of flowers, she flies back to the hive and does something remarkable, she dances. We call it the waggle dance. So let's look at how it works. The bee walks in a straight line while shaking her abdomen, then loops back and repeats it, tracing a kind of figure-eight. And here's what's interesting: the direction of that straight run tells the other bees which way to fly relative to the sun. If she waggles straight up, that means fly toward the sun. The angle off vertical matches the angle off the sun. And the length of the waggle? That encodes distance, the longer she waggles, the farther the food. Now, think about what this means. A tiny insect is essentially giving her sisters a map, using nothing but movement in the dark of the hive. Researchers confirmed this by training bees to feeders and then watching the dances predict exactly where other bees flew. It's one of the few known examples of symbolic communication outside of humans.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How honeybees use a dance to communicate the location of food
Why honeybees prefer flowers that are close to the hive
How the sun affects the daily activity of honeybees
The differences between honeybees and other insects
questions.why: 整段講稿聚焦於蜜蜂如何用搖擺舞傳達食物位置。

questions.q: According to the professor, what does the length of the waggle indicate?
options: The quality of the flowers
The direction toward the sun
The distance to the food
The number of bees needed
questions.why: 講者明確指出搖擺時間越長,食物越遠,代表距離。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the inside of the hive?
options: It is brightly lit so bees can see the dance clearly
Bees can interpret the dance without seeing the sun directly
The dance is actually performed outside near the flowers
Only the oldest bees are able to perform the dance
questions.why: 蜂巢內漆黑,蜜蜂靠動作與重力理解方向,無需直接看到太陽。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention training bees to feeders?
options: To show that bees can be domesticated like other animals
To explain why bees sometimes get lost on the way home
To compare wild bees with laboratory bees
To provide evidence that the dance actually predicts where bees fly
questions.why: 提到訓練實驗是為了證明舞蹈確實能預測蜜蜂的飛行地點。

talk.text: So let's talk about how you actually remember things for the long term. Most students, when they study, cram, they review everything the night before the exam. Now, what's interesting is that psychologists have known for over a century that this is one of the least efficient ways to learn. The better approach is called the spacing effect. The idea is simple: instead of studying for three hours in one sitting, you break it into shorter sessions spread across several days. Same total time, but the memory sticks far better. Why? Well, each time you let a little forgetting happen and then retrieve the information again, you're forcing your brain to rebuild the memory, and that rebuilding strengthens it. Hermann Ebbinghaus first documented this in the 1880s, testing his own memory on lists of nonsense syllables. He found that spaced repetition dramatically slowed his forgetting curve. So the takeaway for you is counterintuitive but powerful: a little forgetting between sessions is not your enemy. It's actually part of what makes the learning last.

questions.q: What is the main point of the talk?
options: Cramming is the fastest way to prepare for an exam
Spreading study sessions over time improves long-term memory
Nonsense syllables are easier to remember than real words
Forgetting always harms academic performance
questions.why: 全段主旨在於分散學習(間隔效應)能提升長期記憶。

questions.q: Who first documented the spacing effect?
options: A group of modern university students
An unnamed team of classroom teachers
A medical doctor studying sleep patterns
Hermann Ebbinghaus in the 1880s
questions.why: 講者提到 Ebbinghaus 於 1880 年代首次記錄此現象。

questions.q: What does the professor imply about a small amount of forgetting?
options: It can actually help strengthen a memory when you retrieve it again
It permanently erases the information from the brain
It only happens to students who study poorly
It has no measurable effect on learning at all
questions.why: 講者暗示少量遺忘後再提取,反而能強化記憶。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention studying for three hours in one sitting?
options: To recommend it as the single best study strategy
To describe exactly how Ebbinghaus ran his experiments
To contrast an inefficient method with the spaced approach
To argue that longer study is always more effective
questions.why: 提到一次讀三小時是為了對比,凸顯分散學習的優勢。

talk.text: Today let's look at a concept in ecology called the keystone species. Now, a keystone, in an archway, is the stone at the top that holds everything else in place. Remove it, and the whole arch collapses. Ecologists borrowed that word for species that hold an entire ecosystem together. The classic example is the sea otter. So, sea otters love to eat sea urchins. And sea urchins, in turn, love to eat kelp, the giant underwater plants that form these lush kelp forests along the Pacific coast. Now here's the chain. When otters are around, they keep the urchin population in check, and the kelp forests flourish, providing habitat for hundreds of other species, fish, crabs, seals. But when otters were hunted nearly to extinction for their fur, the urchins exploded in number. They mowed down the kelp, leaving what scientists grimly call urchin barrens, bare stretches of seafloor. So the otter, a single species, controls the fate of the whole community. That's what makes it a keystone.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How sea otters were once hunted for their valuable fur
The daily feeding habits of sea urchins
How a single species can control an entire ecosystem
The engineering behind ancient stone archways
questions.why: 主旨在於關鍵物種概念:單一物種如何掌控整個生態系。

questions.q: What happened when sea otters were nearly wiped out?
options: Sea urchin populations exploded and destroyed the kelp forests
The kelp forests grew larger and denser than before
Fish and seals suddenly became far more numerous
Other predators immediately moved in to replace the otters
questions.why: 海獺幾乎滅絕後,海膽數量暴增並吃光海帶林。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the many species living in kelp forests?
options: They feed directly on the sea otters themselves
They are completely unaffected by changes in otter numbers
They actually prefer urchin barrens to kelp forests
Their survival depends indirectly on the presence of sea otters
questions.why: 許多物種依賴海帶林棲地,而海帶林又靠海獺維持,故間接依賴海獺。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe a keystone in an archway?
options: To explain the history of how archways are built
To offer an analogy for how one species supports an ecosystem
To show that otters like to live near stone structures
To compare kelp to human building materials
questions.why: 拱門拱心石是類比,用來說明單一物種如何支撐整個生態系。

talk.text: Alright, I want to try a little demonstration with you. Imagine I show you the word red, but it's printed in blue ink, and I ask you to name the ink color, not read the word, just say the color. Go ahead and try it. Most people stumble. They want to say red because that's what the word says. This little delay is called the Stroop effect, named after John Ridley Stroop, who studied it in the 1930s. So what's going on? Well, for a literate adult, reading is automatic. You can't not read a word you see, it happens instantly, without effort. Naming a color, on the other hand, takes a bit more deliberate attention. So when the word and the ink color conflict, your automatic reading response interferes with the slower color-naming task, and your brain has to work to suppress it. That's why you hesitate. Now, what makes this so useful is that it gives psychologists a clean, measurable window into attention and automatic processing. The Stroop task is still used in labs and clinics today.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: Why the color blue is harder to see than red
How young children first learn to read
The history of ink and modern printing
A task that reveals how automatic reading interferes with attention
questions.why: 主旨為 Stroop 效應,說明自動化閱讀如何干擾注意力任務。

questions.q: According to the professor, why is reading described as automatic?
options: Because it requires deliberate effort each and every time
Because a literate adult reads a word instantly, without trying
Because it is always slower than naming colors
Because only trained experts are able to do it
questions.why: 講者指出識字成人看到字就會即刻自動閱讀,無需刻意。

questions.q: What can be inferred about someone who cannot yet read?
options: They might not experience the Stroop delay as strongly
They would find the color-naming task completely impossible
They would name colors much more slowly than readers
They would always say the wrong color out loud
questions.why: 既然干擾來自自動閱讀,不識字者較不會有此延遲。

questions.q: Why does the professor begin with a demonstration?
options: To test whether the students in the room can read
To show that the effect only works with written words
To let students personally experience the effect before explaining it
To prove that most of the students are colorblind
questions.why: 開場示範讓學生親身體驗效應,再進行解釋。

talk.text: So, every autumn, billions of birds set off on migrations, some flying thousands of miles to places they've never been. And here's the puzzle that's fascinated biologists for decades: how do they find their way? Now, part of the answer is what you'd expect, birds use the sun and the stars as compasses. But what's really remarkable is that many species can also sense the Earth's magnetic field. This is called magnetoreception. Let's look at the evidence. In classic experiments, researchers put migratory robins in cages surrounded by artificial magnetic coils. When they shifted the direction of the magnetic field, the birds tried to hop in a correspondingly shifted direction, even though they couldn't see the sky. So the field itself was guiding them. Now, how they actually detect it is still debated. One leading idea is that special molecules in the bird's eye undergo chemical reactions that are sensitive to magnetic fields, meaning the bird might literally see the field as a visual pattern. It's a stunning thought: a compass built right into the eye.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How migratory birds may sense Earth's magnetic field to navigate
Why birds choose to migrate in autumn rather than in spring
How far the various bird species are able to fly
The role that the stars once played in human navigation
questions.why: 主旨為候鳥如何以感測地磁來導航。

questions.q: In the cage experiments, what did the robins do when the magnetic field was shifted?
options: They stopped moving around entirely
They flew straight toward the artificial light
They ignored the change to the field completely
They tried to hop in a correspondingly shifted direction
questions.why: 當磁場方向改變,知更鳥便朝相應改變的方向跳動。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the fact that the birds oriented without seeing the sky?
options: The birds must have relied only on the stars
The magnetic field alone can provide directional information
The birds were simply guessing at random
The experiment ultimately failed to show anything useful
questions.why: 鳥看不到天空仍能定向,推知磁場本身即可提供方向資訊。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention molecules in the bird's eye?
options: To prove that birds actually have very poor eyesight
To explain why birds tend to fly mostly at night
To describe a leading hypothesis for how birds detect the field
To compare the structure of bird eyes with human eyes
questions.why: 提到眼中分子是為了說明鳥如何偵測磁場的主要假說。

talk.text: Here's something that feels impossible until you experience it. You're looking right at a scene, and something big changes, and you completely fail to notice. Psychologists call this change blindness. So let's look at a famous study. Researchers had an experimenter stop a stranger on campus to ask for directions. While they were talking, two people carrying a door rudely walked between them. And in that brief moment, the original experimenter was swapped out for a completely different person, different height, different clothes, different voice. And here's the striking part: about half the people never noticed they were now talking to a stranger. Now, why does this happen? Well, it turns out our vision doesn't work like a video camera storing every detail. Instead, we hold onto just enough of a scene to get by, and we assume the rest stays stable. Unless something grabs our attention at the exact moment of change, we simply overwrite the old information without comparing. So change blindness reveals something deep: we see far less of the world, moment to moment, than we think we do.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: People generally have excellent memories for faces
Human vision works exactly like a video camera
People often fail to notice large changes in a visual scene
Asking a stranger for directions is usually confusing
questions.why: 主旨為變化盲視:人常察覺不到視覺場景中的大變化。

questions.q: In the study, what happened while the door passed between the two people?
options: The stranger quietly walked away from the scene
The original experimenter was replaced by a different person
The door was left behind on the sidewalk
The whole conversation was secretly recorded on camera
questions.why: 門經過時,原本的實驗者被換成另一個人。

questions.q: What can be inferred about how we store visual scenes?
options: We remember every single detail perfectly
We store scenes like high-resolution photographs
We carefully compare every new image to the last one
We keep only enough detail to function and assume the rest is stable
questions.why: 推知我們只保留足以應付的細節,並假設其餘不變。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that vision does not work like a video camera?
options: To explain why people miss changes they are looking right at
To argue that cameras are more reliable than human eyes
To describe the equipment used to film the study
To suggest people should record their own conversations
questions.why: 以視覺不像攝影機來解釋為何人會漏看正注視的變化。

talk.text: Okay, when you walk through a forest, you probably think of the trees as individuals, each competing for light and water. But underground, something very different is going on. So let's look at what's beneath your feet. The roots of most trees are wrapped in threads of fungus, forming a partnership called a mycorrhiza. The fungus extends a vast web of tiny filaments through the soil, far beyond what the roots could reach on their own. Now, what's interesting is that these fungal threads don't just connect to one tree, they link many trees together into a shared network. Some scientists nickname it the wood wide web. And through this network, trees can actually exchange resources. A big tree in full sunlight can send sugars through the fungus to a shaded seedling that can't yet make enough of its own. In return, the fungus takes a cut of the sugar as payment, and helps the trees absorb water and nutrients like phosphorus. So the forest isn't just a crowd of competitors. In a real sense, it's a connected, cooperating community.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How trees compete for sunlight high in the canopy
How underground fungal networks connect and support trees
Why forests need more water than other habitats
How seedlings grow best in full open sunlight
questions.why: 主旨為地下真菌網路如何連結並支持樹木。

questions.q: What does the fungus receive in return for its help?
options: A share of the sugars the trees produce
Sunlight filtered down from the forest canopy
Protection from insects and grazing animals
Seeds that it can spread through the soil
questions.why: 真菌以協助換取樹木製造的部分糖分作為報酬。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a shaded seedling connected to the network?
options: It will always die quickly without any sunlight
It competes directly against the fungus for food
It may survive by receiving sugars from larger trees
It produces far more sugar than a large tree does
questions.why: 推知遮蔭的幼苗可藉由網路從大樹獲得糖分而存活。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the nickname wood wide web?
options: To argue that forests are actually connected to the internet
To criticize scientists for using casual slang terms
To explain the way that fungi reproduce underground
To vividly convey that many trees are linked into one network
questions.why: 用「木維網」這綽號生動傳達多棵樹連成一個網路。

talk.text: Think about a moment when you heard shocking news, maybe a major public event. You can probably recall not just the news itself, but where you were, who told you, what you were doing. Psychologists call these vivid, detailed recollections flashbulb memories, as if a camera flash froze the whole scene. Now, for a long time people assumed these memories were especially accurate, precisely because they feel so sharp and confident. But here's the twist. When researchers actually tested this, they asked people to record their memory of a shocking event the day after it happened, then interviewed them again years later. And what they found is that the memories had drifted, details changed, sometimes dramatically, even though people remained absolutely certain they were correct. So the lesson is subtle. Flashbulb memories are special, but not in the way we thought. They're not more accurate; they're more vivid and more confidently held. The emotional intensity of the moment fools us into trusting them. And that gap, between confidence and accuracy, is one of the most important ideas in memory research.

questions.q: What is the main point of the talk?
options: Shocking public events are actually very easy to forget
Cameras help people remember important events better
Public events are more important to recall than personal ones
Vivid emotional memories feel accurate but often are not
questions.why: 主旨為閃光燈記憶雖鮮明卻未必準確。

questions.q: How did researchers test the accuracy of flashbulb memories?
options: By showing participants photographs of the event afterward
By measuring the brain activity of the participants
By recording memories soon after the event and re-checking years later
By asking participants to predict future news events
questions.why: 研究者在事件後不久記錄記憶,多年後再核對。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the relationship between confidence and accuracy?
options: A person can be very confident yet still remember incorrectly
Confidence in a memory always guarantees it is accurate
Truly accurate memories are never held with confidence
Confidence and accuracy are essentially the same thing
questions.why: 推知人可以非常有信心卻仍記錯,信心與準確可脫鉤。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention a camera flash freezing a scene?
options: To explain how photographs are chemically developed
To illustrate why these memories feel so vivid and complete
To argue that people should take more photographs
To describe the equipment used in the research study
questions.why: 相機閃光凍結畫面的比喻,說明這類記憶為何如此鮮明完整。

talk.text: So imagine trying to catch a flying insect in complete darkness, at high speed, without bumping into anything. That's what many bats do every single night. And they pull it off using echolocation. Here's how it works. The bat sends out a stream of high-pitched calls, so high that we can't even hear them. These sounds travel out, bounce off objects, and return as echoes. By measuring how long the echo takes to come back, the bat calculates how far away something is. And by comparing the echo in its two ears, it works out the direction. Now, what's really impressive is the detail. As a bat closes in on a moth, it speeds up its calls into a rapid buzz, getting constant updates on the moth's exact position, right up to the moment of capture. But here's a twist in the story, some moths have evolved a counter-strategy. They can hear the bat's calls coming and take evasive dives, or even produce their own clicks to jam the bat's sonar. So it's a genuine evolutionary arms race, playing out in the dark above our heads.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How bats use sound echoes to locate prey in the dark
Why bats are active only during the daytime
How moths reproduce during the summer months
Why humans cannot hear very high-pitched sounds
questions.why: 主旨為蝙蝠如何用回聲在黑暗中定位獵物。

questions.q: How does a bat determine the direction of an object?
options: By detecting the object's color in the dark
By comparing the echo received in its two ears
By sensing the temperature of the surrounding air
By following the scent trail left by the moth
questions.why: 蝙蝠靠比較兩耳收到的回聲差異來判斷方向。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the relationship between bats and some moths?
options: The moths are completely defenseless against the bats
Bats and moths essentially never interact at all
The moths actively help the bats find their food
Both have evolved strategies in response to each other
questions.why: 蝙蝠與部分蛾各自演化出攻防策略,形成演化軍備競賽。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that a bat speeds up its calls into a buzz?
options: To show that bats become tired very quickly
To explain why we can suddenly start to hear the bat
To illustrate how the bat gets rapid updates as it nears prey
To describe how bats communicate with one another
questions.why: 叫聲加速成嗡鳴,是為了說明逼近獵物時能快速更新位置。

talk.text: Let's talk about a mental habit that affects every one of us, whether we like it or not. It's called confirmation bias. Basically, once we hold a belief, we tend to seek out and favor information that supports it, while ignoring or dismissing evidence that contradicts it. Now, let's look at a classic experiment. Researchers gave people a series of numbers, say, two, four, six, and asked them to figure out the hidden rule. Most people guessed the rule was even numbers going up by two, and then they only tested sequences that fit that guess, like eight, ten, twelve. And of course, those all worked, so they felt confident. But here's the thing: the actual rule was just any three increasing numbers. Because people only tested cases they expected to confirm, they never tried a sequence that could prove them wrong. And that's the trap. To really test an idea, you have to look for evidence that could break it, not just evidence that props it up. This is why confirmation bias is so dangerous, it feels like careful thinking, when it's actually the opposite.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: People are generally very good at solving number puzzles
Even numbers are easier to remember than odd ones
People tend to favor evidence that supports what they already believe
Scientific experiments always produce reliable results
questions.why: 主旨為確認偏誤:人偏好支持既有信念的證據。

questions.q: What was the actual hidden rule in the experiment?
options: Even numbers going up by two each time
Numbers that add up to a total of twelve
The same number repeated exactly three times
Any three increasing numbers
questions.why: 實驗真正的規則其實是「任何三個遞增的數字」。

questions.q: What does the professor suggest is the best way to test an idea?
options: Repeat the same tests that have already worked
Look for evidence that could prove the idea wrong
Ask other people what they happen to believe
Avoid testing the idea at all if possible
questions.why: 講者建議要尋找可能推翻想法的證據才是真正的檢驗。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe the number experiment?
options: To give a concrete example of confirmation bias in action
To teach students some advanced mathematics
To show that most people are poor at basic counting
To prove that the hidden rule was fundamentally unfair
questions.why: 數字實驗是具體例子,示範確認偏誤如何運作。

talk.text: Alright, here's a discovery that happened partly by accident. In the 1990s, researchers were recording from individual neurons in a monkey's brain, specifically, cells that fired when the monkey reached for a piece of food. But then something strange happened. One of the researchers reached for the food himself, and the very same neuron in the watching monkey fired, even though the monkey wasn't moving at all. So the cell responded both when the monkey performed an action and when it merely saw someone else perform it. These came to be called mirror neurons. Now, why is this such a big deal? Well, it suggests the brain understands other people's actions by internally simulating them, as if to grasp what you're doing, my brain quietly runs the same program it would use to do it myself. And some scientists have gone further, proposing that mirror neurons might underlie empathy and imitation, helping us learn skills by watching and even feel what others feel. Now, I should be careful here, a lot of that is still debated. But the core finding remains one of the most intriguing in neuroscience.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How monkeys typically reach for pieces of food
Neurons that fire both when acting and when watching others act
Why accidents are so common in research laboratories
How to record faint electrical signals from cells
questions.why: 主旨為鏡像神經元:自己動作與看別人動作時都會激發的神經元。

questions.q: What surprised the researchers during the experiment?
options: The monkey suddenly refused to eat any food
The neuron they were recording from stopped working
A neuron fired when the monkey only watched someone reach for food
The monkey imitated the researcher almost immediately
questions.why: 讓研究者驚訝的是,猴子只是看人拿食物,神經元就激發了。

questions.q: What does the discovery suggest about how the brain understands actions?
options: The brain may understand actions by internally simulating them
The brain simply ignores the actions of other individuals
The brain can only ever understand its own movements
The brain stores actions as a set of written instructions
questions.why: 推知大腦可能藉由內在模擬他人動作來理解該動作。

questions.q: Why does the professor say he needs to be careful here?
options: Because the experiment itself was physically dangerous
Because monkeys can sometimes become aggressive
Because the neurons are extremely hard to see
Because some claims about mirror neurons are still debated
questions.why: 講者提醒謹慎,因為關於鏡像神經元的部分主張仍有爭議。

talk.text: So let's go back to one of the most famous experiments in the history of psychology. In the early 1900s, a Russian physiologist named Ivan Pavlov was actually studying digestion in dogs. He was measuring how much they salivated when given food. But he noticed something he didn't expect: the dogs started drooling before the food even arrived, as soon as they heard the footsteps of the assistant who fed them. Now, Pavlov realized he'd stumbled onto something important. So he ran a careful test. He began ringing a bell just before feeding the dogs. At first, the bell meant nothing to them. But after pairing the bell with food again and again, the dogs began to salivate at the sound of the bell alone, with no food present. This is what we call classical conditioning, an animal learns to associate a neutral signal with something meaningful, until the signal itself triggers the response. It sounds simple, but this basic form of learning turned out to be everywhere, shaping how animals, and people, form associations, habits, and even fears.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How dogs physically digest the food they eat
The complete life story of Ivan Pavlov
Why dogs generally make such good household pets
How animals learn to link a neutral signal with a meaningful event
questions.why: 主旨為古典制約:動物如何將中性訊號與有意義事件連結。

questions.q: What was Pavlov originally studying?
options: Digestion and salivation in dogs
The general hearing ability of dogs
How to train dogs to ring bells on command
The range of emotions that dogs feel
questions.why: Pavlov 原本研究的是狗的消化與唾液分泌。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the bell before the conditioning began?
options: It clearly frightened the dogs
It already made the dogs salivate on its own
It had no special meaning to the dogs
It was much louder than the assistant's footsteps
questions.why: 制約前鈴聲對狗毫無特殊意義,故不會引發唾液。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the dogs drooling at the assistant's footsteps?
options: To show that the dogs had been poorly trained
To describe the accidental observation that led to the discovery
To explain the reason the assistant was later fired
To prove that dogs strongly dislike hearing footsteps
questions.why: 提到狗聽到腳步聲流口水,是引出這項發現的意外觀察。

talk.text: Okay, let's talk about an animal that might be the closest thing to alien intelligence on our own planet, the octopus. Now, octopuses are mollusks, cousins of clams and snails, which makes their intelligence all the more surprising. So let's look at what they can do. In the lab, octopuses can solve puzzles, open jars to reach food, and even learn by watching. Some have been known to escape their tanks at night, cross the floor, and sneak into a neighboring tank for a snack. But maybe their most stunning ability is camouflage. An octopus can change the color and even the texture of its skin in a fraction of a second, blending into rock, sand, or coral almost perfectly. And here's the truly strange part: octopuses are colorblind, as far as we can tell, they can't see color at all. So how do they match colors they can't perceive? One hypothesis is that their skin itself contains light-sensitive cells. Honestly, we still don't fully understand it. And that's part of what makes the octopus such a fascinating puzzle.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The surprising intelligence and camouflage abilities of octopuses
How clams and snails are biologically related
Why octopuses are usually kept in laboratory tanks
The typical diet of large ocean predators
questions.why: 主旨為章魚令人驚訝的智力與偽裝能力。

questions.q: What is described as the octopus's most stunning ability?
options: Opening sealed jars to reach food
Escaping from its tank at night
Rapidly changing the color and texture of its skin
Swimming at extremely high speeds
questions.why: 講者稱章魚最驚人的能力是瞬間改變皮膚顏色與質地。

questions.q: Why is the octopus's camouflage described as a puzzle?
options: Because octopuses very rarely actually use it
Because it only works during the nighttime
Because other animals are able to do it much better
Because octopuses match colors they apparently cannot see
questions.why: 章魚偽裝成謎,因為牠們能配對自己似乎看不見的顏色。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that octopuses are mollusks?
options: To explain what kind of food they eat
To emphasize how surprising their intelligence is
To compare their size to that of snails
To describe the ocean habitats where they live
questions.why: 提到章魚屬軟體動物,是為了凸顯其智力有多令人意外。

talk.text: Here's a question that sounds almost backwards: can the expression on your face actually change how you feel? Most of us assume it goes one way, you feel happy, so you smile. But the facial feedback hypothesis proposes that it also works in reverse: the act of smiling can make you feel a little happier. So let's look at how psychologists tested this in a clever way. They didn't want to just tell people to smile, because then people might guess the point and act happier on purpose. So instead, they had participants hold a pen in their mouth. For one group, holding the pen between the teeth forced the face into something like a smile. For another group, holding it with the lips did the opposite. Then everyone rated how funny they found some cartoons. And the smiling group tended to rate the cartoons as funnier. Now, I'll note that some later studies have had trouble reproducing this, so it's an ongoing debate. But the core idea is striking: emotion may not just flow from the mind to the body. The body can talk back to the mind.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: Cartoons are actually funnier than people realize
Smiling is mainly a sign of good manners
Facial expressions may influence how we feel, not just reflect it
Holding a pen in the mouth improves concentration
questions.why: 主旨為臉部回饋假說:表情可能反過來影響情緒。

questions.q: Why did researchers have participants hold a pen in their mouth?
options: To measure how fast the participants could read
To shape the face into a smile or frown without telling them why
To keep the participants from talking during the study
To test the participants' sense of taste
questions.why: 用筆是為了在不告知目的下,讓臉部形成微笑或皺眉。

questions.q: Why did the researchers avoid simply asking people to smile?
options: People might guess the purpose and change their behavior
Smiling on command is physically impossible for most people
The participants strongly disliked being asked to smile
Doing so would have taken far too much time
questions.why: 直接要人微笑,受試者可能猜到目的而刻意改變行為。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that some studies failed to reproduce the result?
options: To prove that the hypothesis is completely false
To criticize the original researchers for their methods
To suggest that students should avoid smiling
To acknowledge that the idea is still being debated
questions.why: 提到後續研究難以重現,是坦承此假說仍有爭議。

talk.text: So those brilliant colors you see in a healthy coral reef, the reef itself isn't really the source. Here's what's going on. Coral animals have tiny algae living inside their tissues, and these algae are what give coral much of its color. Now, this is a partnership, a symbiosis. The algae use sunlight to make sugars, and they share those sugars with the coral, providing most of the coral's food. In return, the coral gives the algae a safe, sunlit place to live. It's a beautiful deal that built the largest living structures on Earth. But here's the problem. When the water gets too warm, even by just a degree or two for a sustained period, the partnership breaks down. The stressed coral expels the algae. And without the algae, the coral loses both its color and its main food source, turning ghostly white. This is what we call coral bleaching. A bleached coral isn't dead yet, if conditions improve quickly, the algae can return. But if the heat lasts too long, the coral starves. And that's why rising ocean temperatures are such a threat to reefs.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why coral reefs are brightly colored purely for beauty
How algae reproduce throughout the ocean
The coral-algae partnership and what happens when it breaks down
How large ocean structures are built by humans
questions.why: 主旨為珊瑚與藻類的共生關係及其崩解(白化)。

questions.q: What do the algae provide to the coral?
options: Most of the coral's food, in the form of sugars
Physical protection from ocean predators
A hard protective outer shell
Cooler water temperatures around the reef
questions.why: 藻類以糖分形式提供珊瑚大部分的食物。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a coral that has just bleached?
options: It is already completely dead
It may recover if conditions improve soon
It can never regain its original color
It no longer has any need for the algae
questions.why: 剛白化的珊瑚尚未死亡,若環境迅速改善仍可能恢復。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that the water only needs to warm by a degree or two?
options: To show that coral actually prefers very warm water
To explain how the algae manage to make sugar
To describe how the size of reefs is measured
To emphasize how sensitive the partnership is to heat
questions.why: 強調只需升溫一兩度,凸顯這共生關係對高溫多麼敏感。

talk.text: Alright, let's talk about the part of your memory you're using right this second, working memory. This is the mental workspace where you hold and manipulate information for a few seconds, like keeping a phone number in mind while you dial it. Now, here's the catch: working memory is surprisingly limited. A famous paper from the 1950s suggested we can hold only about seven items at once, and more recent estimates put it even lower, around four. So how do we ever manage complex thoughts? The trick is called chunking. Let me show you. Try to hold these letters: F, B, I, C, I, A, N, A, S, A. Ten separate letters, that's tough. But notice they're actually FBI, CIA, NASA. Grouped that way, it's just three chunks, and suddenly it's easy. So chunking lets us package many small items into a few meaningful units, effectively expanding what we can hold. This is exactly how experts handle huge amounts of information, a chess master doesn't see twenty separate pieces, but a few familiar patterns. So the limit isn't really about raw quantity. It's about how well you organize what you're trying to hold.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: Phone numbers are inherently hard to remember
Working memory has essentially unlimited capacity
Experts are born with physically larger brains
Working memory is limited, but chunking expands what we can hold
questions.why: 主旨為工作記憶有限,但可用組塊法擴充容量。

questions.q: According to more recent estimates, about how many items can working memory hold?
options: About ten items
About four items
About twenty items
An essentially unlimited number of items
questions.why: 較新的估計指出工作記憶約只能容納四個項目。

questions.q: What can be inferred about how a chess master views the board?
options: They group pieces into familiar patterns rather than seeing each separately
They memorize every single piece one at a time
They cannot really remember the positions at all
They rely almost entirely on luck
questions.why: 推知棋王把棋子看成熟悉的組型,而非逐一記憶。

questions.q: Why does the professor present the letters F, B, I, C, I, A, N, A, S, A?
options: To quietly test the students' spelling ability
To show that letters are easier to recall than numbers
To demonstrate how chunking makes information easier to hold
To explain the history of government agencies
questions.why: 列出這些字母是為了示範組塊如何讓資訊更易記住。

talk.text: Have you ever noticed that even without an alarm clock, your body seems to know roughly when to wake up? That's because you have an internal clock. So let's look at circadian rhythms, these are the roughly 24-hour cycles that govern sleep, alertness, body temperature, and hormone release in almost every living thing. Now, deep in your brain sits a tiny cluster of cells that acts as the master clock, keeping the rest of your body on schedule. But here's what's interesting: this clock doesn't run on a perfect 24-hour cycle on its own. Left in total darkness, with no time cues, people's rhythms drift, often to a cycle slightly longer than 24 hours. So the clock needs to be reset each day, and the main signal it uses is light. When morning light hits your eyes, it tells the master clock to resync to the outside world. This is exactly why jet lag happens, you fly across time zones, and for a few days your internal clock is still set to where you came from, out of step with the local sun. Light is what eventually pulls it back in line.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The body's internal clock and how light keeps it on schedule
Why people generally need to use alarm clocks
How to permanently cure common sleep disorders
The exact length of a single day on Earth
questions.why: 主旨為生理時鐘(晝夜節律)及光如何使其對時。

questions.q: What happens to people's rhythms in total darkness with no time cues?
options: The rhythms stop running completely
The rhythms speed up to a 12-hour cycle
The rhythms stay exactly 24 hours long
The rhythms drift, often to slightly longer than 24 hours
questions.why: 在完全黑暗無時間線索下,節律會漂移,常略長於 24 小時。

questions.q: Based on the talk, why does jet lag occur?
options: Because airplane cabins are far too bright
Because people tend to sleep too much while flying
Because the internal clock is still set to the departure time zone
Because the master clock stops working entirely during the flight
questions.why: 時差是因內在時鐘仍停留在出發地時區,與當地不同步。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention waking up without an alarm clock?
options: To recommend that people stop using alarms
To introduce the idea that the body has an internal clock
To explain why alarm clocks are often unreliable
To describe the setup of a particular sleep experiment
questions.why: 以不靠鬧鐘也能醒來,引入身體有內在時鐘的概念。

talk.topic: The visual cliff and depth perception
talk.text: So here's a question developmental psychologists wanted to answer: are we born able to perceive depth, or do we have to learn it? To test this, researchers in the 1960s built something called the visual cliff. Picture a table with a sturdy glass surface on top. On one half, a checkered pattern sits right under the glass, so it looks solid. But on the other half, the same pattern is placed on the floor far below the glass, so even though the glass is perfectly safe to crawl on, it looks like a sudden drop-off, a cliff. Then they placed crawling infants on the shallow side and had their mothers call to them from across the deep side. And here's what they found: most babies would crawl to the edge, and then stop. They refused to cross over the apparent cliff, even to reach their own mothers. So this suggested that by the time babies can crawl, they can already perceive depth and sense danger. It's a wonderfully simple design, no drop at all, completely safe, yet it revealed something profound about how early we come to see the world in three dimensions.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: Babies greatly enjoy crawling around on glass surfaces
Mothers can always calm their crying babies down
An experiment tested whether infants can perceive depth
Checkered patterns tend to confuse young children
questions.why: 主旨為視覺懸崖實驗,測試嬰兒是否能感知深度。

questions.q: What did most infants do when placed on the shallow side?
options: They immediately crawled straight across the deep side
They crawled to the edge but refused to cross the apparent cliff
They fell right through the glass surface
They ignored their mothers and fell asleep
questions.why: 多數嬰兒爬到邊緣就停下,拒絕越過看似懸崖之處。

questions.q: What does the experiment suggest about depth perception?
options: It is present at least by the time babies can crawl
It develops only much later in adulthood
It cannot really be tested in a laboratory
It depends entirely on the color of the floor
questions.why: 推知深度知覺至少在嬰兒會爬時就已存在。

questions.q: Why does the professor emphasize that the glass was completely safe?
options: To warn the students about the dangers of broken glass
To explain in detail how the table was constructed
To suggest that the babies were actually in real danger
To make clear the babies reacted to the appearance of depth, not real risk
questions.why: 強調玻璃安全,說明嬰兒是對「看起來的深度」反應,而非真實危險。

talk.text: So consider an ant colony finding the shortest path to a pile of food. There's no boss ant directing traffic, no blueprint, no ant that even understands the whole map. And yet the colony as a whole solves the problem beautifully. How? So let's look at the mechanism. When an ant finds food, it lays down a chemical trail, a pheromone, as it carries a crumb back to the nest. Other ants tend to follow pheromone trails, and when they do, they add their own scent, making the trail stronger. Now here's the clever part. A shorter path gets walked more quickly, so ants travel back and forth on it more often in the same amount of time. That means the pheromone builds up faster on the short path than on a long one. And the stronger the trail, the more ants it attracts, a feedback loop that steadily concentrates the colony on the best route. Scientists call this kind of indirect coordination stigmergy, individuals communicating by changing their shared environment, rather than talking directly. And it's so effective that computer scientists have borrowed it to solve routing problems in networks.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How a single leader ant directs the whole colony
How ants collectively find short paths without central control
Why ants tend to prefer sweet kinds of food
How ants construct their underground nests
questions.why: 主旨為螞蟻如何在無中央指揮下集體找出短路徑。

questions.q: How does an ant mark a path to food?
options: By laying down a chemical pheromone trail
By leaving behind visible footprints
By making a distinctive sound
By dropping small bits of the food
questions.why: 螞蟻靠沿途留下化學費洛蒙來標記通往食物的路徑。

questions.q: Why does the pheromone build up faster on a shorter path?
options: Because shorter paths simply have more food nearby
Because ants avoid the longer paths entirely
Because the chemical is naturally stronger on short paths
Because ants complete more round trips on it in the same time
questions.why: 短路徑同時間內能來回更多趟,故費洛蒙累積更快。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that computer scientists borrowed this idea?
options: To argue that ants are actually smarter than computers
To explain how the pheromone chemical is produced
To show that the strategy is powerful enough to solve real-world problems
To describe how ants have learned to use technology
questions.why: 提到電腦科學家借用此法,說明該策略強大到能解決實際問題。

talk.text: Today I want to talk about superconductivity, one of the most striking phenomena in modern physics. In an ordinary metal wire, electrons collide with vibrating atoms, and these collisions create resistance, which wastes energy as heat. But in 1911, a Dutch physicist cooled mercury to about four degrees above absolute zero and discovered something remarkable: its electrical resistance vanished completely. Below a certain critical temperature, the material became a superconductor, carrying current with no energy loss at all. The explanation, worked out decades later, is that electrons pair up and move through the crystal in a coordinated way, so they no longer scatter. Now, here's why this matters. Superconducting magnets are already used in hospital MRI machines and in particle accelerators. The catch is temperature. Traditional superconductors only work when cooled with expensive liquid helium. So researchers are hunting for materials that superconduct at higher temperatures, ideally at room temperature. If they succeed, we could see lossless power grids and levitating trains become ordinary technology.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: A phenomenon in which certain materials conduct electricity without resistance
The history of the discovery of mercury as a metal
How MRI machines are used to diagnose diseases
Why ordinary metal wires heat up when carrying current
questions.why: 整段講稿以超導現象為核心,說明電阻消失、成因與應用,故主旨為某些材料能無電阻導電。其他選項僅為文中細節或延伸,非主旨。

questions.q: According to the professor, what did the Dutch physicist observe in 1911?
options: Mercury melted at four degrees above absolute zero
The electrical resistance of mercury disappeared completely
Electrons stopped moving through the wire entirely
The mercury began to glow brightly
questions.why: 講稿明確指出 1911 年將水銀冷卻到約絕對零度以上四度時,其電阻完全消失。選項提到熔化、電子停止或發光皆非文中所述。

questions.q: What can be inferred about current traditional superconductors?
options: They already work best at room temperature
They are cheaper to use than ordinary copper wire
Their need for extreme cooling limits their everyday use
They have no practical applications so far
questions.why: 講稿提到傳統超導體須用昂貴的液態氦冷卻,且研究者正尋找更高溫材料,可推論極低溫需求限制了其日常普及。文中亦說已用於 MRI,故非毫無應用。

questions.q: How does the professor organize the talk?
options: By comparing several competing scientific theories
By describing a laboratory experiment step by step
By listing superconductors in order of critical temperature
By explaining the phenomenon, then its cause, and finally its applications
questions.why: 教授先描述超導現象,接著解釋電子配對的成因,最後談 MRI、電網與磁浮列車等應用,屬「現象—成因—應用」的結構。

talk.text: Let's turn to how stars are born, live, and die. A star begins its life inside a giant cloud of gas and dust called a nebula. Gravity pulls this material together, and as the core grows denser, it heats up. When the temperature reaches about ten million degrees, hydrogen atoms begin fusing into helium, releasing enormous energy. That's the moment a star truly switches on. For most of its life, the star stays in balance: the outward push of fusion energy exactly offsets the inward pull of gravity. Our Sun has been in this stable phase for nearly five billion years. But no star lasts forever. Once the hydrogen fuel runs low, the balance breaks. A star like the Sun swells into a red giant, then sheds its outer layers and leaves behind a dim, dense core called a white dwarf. Much heavier stars end far more violently, exploding as supernovae and scattering heavy elements across space. Remarkably, the carbon in your body and the iron in your blood were forged inside such dying stars.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To explain how nebulae are named by astronomers
To describe the stages a star passes through from birth to death
To argue that the Sun will explode in the near future
To compare the size of the Sun with a white dwarf
questions.why: 講稿依序介紹恆星從星雲誕生、穩定燃燒到死亡的各階段,主旨為描述恆星由生到死的歷程。其他選項僅為片段細節或錯誤陳述。

questions.q: According to the professor, what causes a star to 'switch on'?
options: Gravity pulling the surrounding nebula apart
The star shedding its cooler outer layers
Hydrogen fusing into helium once the core gets hot enough
A nearby supernova explosion igniting it
questions.why: 講稿指出當核心溫度達約一千萬度,氫融合成氦並釋放能量,恆星才真正「啟動」。其餘選項與恆星點燃機制不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the Sun's eventual fate?
options: It will explode as a bright supernova
It will remain completely unchanged forever
It will collapse directly into a black hole
It will eventually become a white dwarf
questions.why: 講稿說像太陽這樣的恆星會膨脹成紅巨星,拋掉外層後留下白矮星;超新星則是更重恆星的結局,故可推論太陽最終將成為白矮星。

questions.q: How is the information in the talk presented?
options: As a sequence of stages over a star's lifetime
As a debate between two rival astronomers
As a ranked list of the largest known stars
As a set of instructions for observing stars
questions.why: 教授依時間順序敘述恆星生命的各個階段,屬時間序列的組織方式,而非辯論、清單或操作步驟。

talk.text: I'd like to introduce the theory of plate tectonics, which reshaped how we understand our planet. The Earth's outer shell is not a single solid piece. Instead, it is broken into about a dozen large slabs called plates, which float on the hot, slowly flowing rock beneath them. These plates creep along at roughly the speed your fingernails grow, only a few centimeters a year. Where plates pull apart, molten rock rises to fill the gap and creates new crust, as happens along the mid-ocean ridges. Where plates collide, one may dive beneath the other, or the two may crumple upward to build mountains. The Himalayas, for instance, are still rising because India keeps pushing into Asia. And where plates grind sideways past one another, stress builds until it releases suddenly as an earthquake. Before this theory took hold in the 1960s, geologists had no unified way to explain why earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain ranges cluster where they do. Plate tectonics tied all these observations together into a single elegant framework.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How fingernails and rock happen to grow at similar rates
The reasons the Himalayas have stopped rising
A theory that explains the movement of Earth's outer plates
The detailed chemical composition of molten rock
questions.why: 講稿以板塊構造理論為核心,說明地殼分裂成板塊並移動,以及由此解釋地震、火山與山脈,故主旨為解釋地球外殼板塊運動的理論。

questions.q: According to the professor, why are the Himalayas still rising?
options: Molten rock is steadily filling a gap beneath them
A recent earthquake pushed the whole range upward
Two plates are grinding sideways past each other there
India continues to push into Asia
questions.why: 講稿明確指出喜馬拉雅山仍在上升,是因為印度持續擠向亞洲。其他選項描述的是別種板塊邊界作用,非此例成因。

questions.q: What can be inferred about geology before the 1960s?
options: It lacked a single explanation linking earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountains
It had already proven that the plates were moving
It focused its attention only on mid-ocean ridges
It denied that mountain ranges could ever rise
questions.why: 講稿說在此理論於 1960 年代確立前,地質學家沒有統一方式解釋地震、火山與山脈為何群聚,可推論當時缺乏整合性的解釋。

questions.q: How does the professor mainly organize the explanation?
options: By telling the story of one geologist's whole career
By describing what happens at different types of plate boundaries
By ranking natural disasters from least to most deadly
By comparing the Earth in detail with other planets
questions.why: 教授依板塊邊界的不同類型(張裂、碰撞、錯動)分別說明其作用與結果,屬依類別描述的組織方式。

talk.text: Have you ever noticed how the siren of an ambulance sounds higher as it races toward you and then drops to a lower pitch the instant it passes? That everyday experience is the Doppler effect, and it's the focus of today's talk. Sound travels as waves. When the source of the sound moves toward you, each successive wave is emitted from a slightly closer position, so the waves bunch up. Shorter wavelengths mean a higher frequency, which your ear hears as a higher pitch. Once the source passes and moves away, the waves stretch out, the frequency drops, and the pitch falls. The effect isn't limited to sound. Light behaves the same way. When a distant galaxy moves away from us, its light waves stretch toward the red end of the spectrum, an effect we call redshift. By measuring how much a galaxy's light is shifted, astronomers can calculate how fast it is receding. In fact, this is one of the key pieces of evidence that our universe is expanding.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How modern ambulances are designed and built
Why distant galaxies happen to be red in color
The complete difference between sound and light
A shift in wave frequency caused by motion of the source
questions.why: 講稿以都卜勒效應為主題,說明波源移動造成頻率改變,並延伸到聲音與光。故主旨為由波源運動引起的頻率變化。其他選項僅為例子或誤解。

questions.q: According to the professor, why does an approaching siren sound higher?
options: The sound waves bunch up, raising the frequency
The ambulance turns up the volume of the siren
The waves stretch out and lower the frequency
The air temperature changes near the moving vehicle
questions.why: 講稿說波源靠近時,連續的波被擠壓、波長變短、頻率升高,故聽起來音調較高。其他選項與文中機制不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a galaxy whose light is strongly redshifted?
options: It is moving rapidly toward the Earth
It is receding from us at high speed
It has completely stopped emitting light
It is the closest galaxy to our own
questions.why: 講稿指出遠離的星系其光波被拉長而紅移,紅移越大代表遠離越快,故強烈紅移的星系正高速遠離我們。

questions.q: How does the professor introduce the topic?
options: By defining several technical terms in advance
By presenting statistics about traffic accidents
By starting with a familiar everyday experience
By quoting the words of a famous physicist
questions.why: 教授以救護車警笛聲這個日常經驗開場,再引出都卜勒效應的原理,屬以熟悉情境切入的組織方式。

talk.text: For centuries, we wondered whether planets circled other stars, but we had no way to find them. Today I'll explain the clever method that finally let us detect these distant worlds. The main technique is called the transit method. Imagine watching a star very carefully over many nights. If a planet happens to pass directly between that star and us, it blocks a tiny fraction of the star's light. The star appears to dim ever so slightly, then returns to full brightness once the planet moves on. This dip is minuscule, often less than one percent, but sensitive space telescopes can measure it. By timing how regularly the dips repeat, astronomers work out the planet's orbital period. And by measuring how much light is blocked, they estimate the planet's size, since a bigger planet blocks more light. The Kepler space telescope used exactly this approach to discover thousands of planets. Some of them lie in the so-called habitable zone, the region around a star where temperatures could allow liquid water, and perhaps, life.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: A method used to detect planets orbiting other stars
The history of how the Kepler telescope was built
The reasons liquid water is necessary for life
How ordinary stars produce their own light
questions.why: 講稿主要介紹凌日法如何偵測系外行星,故主旨為偵測繞其他恆星運行之行星的方法。其他選項只是文中提及的次要細節。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens during a transit?
options: The star suddenly grows much brighter
The planet blocks a small fraction of the star's light
The planet emits a bright flash of its own light
The star noticeably changes its color
questions.why: 講稿說行星通過恆星與我們之間時,會遮住恆星一小部分的光,使恆星略微變暗。其他選項與凌日現象不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a planet that causes a larger dip in brightness?
options: It orbits its star much more slowly
It lies much farther from its star
It is probably larger in size
It is more likely to support life
questions.why: 講稿指出行星越大遮住的光越多,故亮度下降越明顯者,推論其體積可能越大。與軌道速度、距離或生命無直接關聯。

questions.q: How does the professor mainly develop the talk?
options: By comparing several rival detection methods in turn
By narrating one single night of careful observation
By listing every exoplanet that has been found
By posing a long-standing question and then explaining how it was answered
questions.why: 教授先提出「其他恆星是否有行星」這個長久疑問,再說明凌日法如何解答,屬「提出問題—解答」的組織結構。

talk.text: Today's topic is ocean currents, the great rivers that flow within the sea. These currents fall into two broad groups. Surface currents are driven mainly by the wind, which drags on the top layer of the water and pushes it along. The Gulf Stream is a famous example; it carries warm water from the tropics up along the eastern coast of North America and across toward Europe. That warmth is why parts of Western Europe stay surprisingly mild for their latitude. But there's a second, deeper system, driven not by wind but by differences in water density. Cold water is denser than warm water, and salty water is denser than fresh. Near the poles, water grows cold and salty, becomes heavy, and sinks toward the ocean floor. It then creeps slowly across the globe before rising again elsewhere. This vast, slow loop is sometimes called the global conveyor belt. Together, surface and deep currents move heat around the planet, and that redistribution of heat is a major force shaping the world's climate.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To explain why Western Europe has a very cold climate
To describe the two main types of ocean currents and their effects
To argue that wind is the only cause of ocean currents
To compare the Gulf Stream with rivers on land
questions.why: 講稿分別說明表層(風驅)與深層(密度驅)兩類洋流及其對氣候的影響,故主旨為描述兩大類洋流及其作用。其他選項與內容不符或僅為片段。

questions.q: According to the professor, what drives the deep ocean currents?
options: The daily rotation of the Earth on its axis
The gravitational pull of the Moon
Differences in water density from temperature and saltiness
Wind blowing steadily across the surface
questions.why: 講稿指出深層洋流由水的密度差異驅動,冷水與鹹水較重而下沉。風驅動的是表層洋流,故正確答案為密度差異。

questions.q: What can be inferred about Western Europe if the Gulf Stream weakened?
options: It would immediately receive far more rainfall
Its rivers would suddenly reverse direction
Its coastal seas would become much saltier
Some regions might become colder
questions.why: 講稿說墨西哥灣流帶來的暖水使西歐氣候偏溫和,可推論若灣流減弱,部分地區可能變冷。其他選項無文中依據。

questions.q: How does the professor organize the talk?
options: By dividing currents into two types and describing each
By tracing the long history of ocean exploration
By listing ocean currents from largest to smallest
By following a single drop of water on its journey
questions.why: 教授把洋流分成表層與深層兩類,分別描述其成因與作用,屬分類說明的組織方式。

talk.text: Let's talk about radioactive decay and a concept called half-life. Some atomic nuclei are unstable. Without any outside trigger, they spontaneously break down, giving off particles or energy and transforming into a different element. We can't predict exactly when any single nucleus will decay; it's fundamentally a matter of probability. But when we look at a huge number of atoms together, a clear pattern emerges. The half-life is the time it takes for half of the atoms in a sample to decay. Suppose a substance has a half-life of one thousand years. After one thousand years, half the original atoms remain. After another thousand, a quarter remain, then an eighth, and so on. Notice that the amount never quite reaches zero; it just keeps halving. This predictable clock is enormously useful. Carbon-14, for example, has a half-life of about fifty-seven hundred years, and archaeologists use it to date ancient bones and charcoal. For much older rocks, geologists rely on elements with far longer half-lives, such as uranium, which lets them measure ages in billions of years.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How archaeologists carefully excavate ancient bones
The reasons uranium is dangerous to handle
Radioactive decay and the concept of half-life
The internal structure of a single atom
questions.why: 講稿以放射性衰變與半衰期為主題,說明其機制與定年應用,故主旨為放射性衰變與半衰期的概念。其他選項僅為應用細節或無關內容。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is a 'half-life'?
options: The time it takes for an unstable nucleus to form
The exact moment a single atom happens to decay
The age of the oldest known rock on Earth
The time for half the atoms in a sample to decay
questions.why: 講稿明確定義半衰期為樣本中一半原子衰變所需的時間。其他選項均非此定義。

questions.q: What can be inferred about dating very old rocks?
options: Elements with longer half-lives are more suitable
Carbon-14 works best for dating them
Their age cannot be measured at all
They contain no radioactive atoms whatsoever
questions.why: 講稿說碳-14 適合較年輕樣本,而更古老的岩石須用半衰期極長的鈾等元素,可推論定年極古老岩石宜用半衰期較長的元素。

questions.q: How does the professor explain the concept of half-life?
options: By comparing several different elements first
By giving a numerical example that halves step by step
By describing a dangerous laboratory accident
By listing the dates of major discoveries
questions.why: 教授以「半衰期一千年」的數值例子,逐步說明剩下二分之一、四分之一、八分之一,藉具體遞減例子闡明概念,屬以實例說明的組織方式。

talk.text: Black holes are among the strangest objects in the universe, and today I want to clear up what they actually are. A black hole is a region where matter has been crushed into such a tiny space that its gravity becomes overwhelming. The boundary around it is called the event horizon. Cross that boundary, and nothing can escape, not even light itself, which is exactly why the object looks black. Now, a common misconception is that black holes roam the galaxy like cosmic vacuum cleaners, sucking in everything nearby. That's not quite right. From a safe distance, a black hole's gravity is no stronger than that of an ordinary star of the same mass. If our Sun were somehow replaced by a black hole of equal mass, the Earth would keep orbiting exactly as it does now. So how do we detect something that emits no light? We watch its effect on nearby matter. As gas spirals toward a black hole, it heats up and blazes with X-rays we can detect. We also observe stars whipping around an invisible companion, betraying the presence of an unseen mass.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To warn that a black hole may soon swallow the Sun
To describe in detail how X-ray telescopes are built
To prove that light can in fact escape black holes
To explain what black holes are and correct a common misconception
questions.why: 講稿說明黑洞的本質與事件視界,並糾正「黑洞會吸走一切」的迷思,故主旨為解釋黑洞並澄清常見誤解。其他選項與內容不符。

questions.q: According to the professor, why does a black hole appear black?
options: Not even light can escape from beyond the event horizon
It is coated with a layer of dark cosmic dust
It absorbs only the red part of the spectrum
It is simply too far away for us to see
questions.why: 講稿指出越過事件視界後連光都無法逃脫,故黑洞看起來是黑的。其他選項均非文中原因。

questions.q: What can be inferred if the Sun were replaced by a black hole of equal mass?
options: The Earth would be pulled in almost immediately
The Earth would continue in its current orbit
The Earth would be flung off into deep space
The Earth would suddenly stop rotating
questions.why: 講稿明說若以等質量黑洞取代太陽,地球會照常運行,可推論地球會維持現有軌道。其他選項與此矛盾。

questions.q: How does the professor address the idea that black holes 'suck in everything'?
options: By agreeing with it and adding vivid examples
By ignoring the idea completely
By identifying it as a misconception and then correcting it
By blaming the idea on early astronomers
questions.why: 教授先點出「黑洞像宇宙吸塵器」是常見迷思,再加以糾正,屬指出誤解並更正的組織方式。

talk.text: Today we'll look at why some volcanoes erupt gently while others explode with tremendous violence. The key factor is the magma, the molten rock beneath the surface, and specifically how thick and sticky it is. Thin, runny magma lets gas bubbles escape easily. When such magma reaches the surface, it simply flows out as rivers of lava, the way we see in Hawaii. These eruptions can be dramatic to watch but are relatively gentle. Thick, sticky magma is a different story. It traps gas bubbles, which cannot escape as pressure builds. Think of shaking a bottle of soda and then opening it suddenly. When that pressure finally releases, the result is a violent explosion that can hurl ash and rock high into the atmosphere. The eruption of Mount St. Helens in 1980 was exactly this type. What makes magma thick or thin is largely its silica content: more silica means stickier magma. So by studying the chemistry of a volcano's rock, geologists can gauge what kind of eruptions it is likely to produce.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why some volcanic eruptions are gentle and others explosive
The geological history of the Hawaiian islands
How geologists safely collect volcanic rock samples
The health dangers of drinking too much soda
questions.why: 講稿主旨在解釋岩漿性質為何使火山噴發有溫和與劇烈之分,故答案為為何有些火山噴發溫和、有些劇烈。其他選項僅為比喻或次要提及。

questions.q: According to the professor, what allows thin magma to erupt gently?
options: It contains no dissolved gas at all
Its gas bubbles can escape easily
It is much cooler than thick magma
It has an unusually high silica content
questions.why: 講稿說稀薄流動的岩漿讓氣泡容易逸出,故噴發較溫和。文中指出高矽含量反而使岩漿黏稠,故其他選項不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about magma with a high silica content?
options: It flows even more easily than runny magma
It never manages to reach the surface
It is more likely to produce explosive eruptions
It contains no dissolved gas of any kind
questions.why: 講稿指出矽含量越高岩漿越黏,而黏稠岩漿會困住氣體導致劇烈爆發,可推論高矽岩漿較易產生爆炸性噴發。

questions.q: How does the professor mainly organize the explanation?
options: By listing famous volcanoes in order of their height
By narrating the 1980 eruption from start to finish
By describing the steps of safely collecting magma
By contrasting two types of magma and the eruptions they cause
questions.why: 教授對比稀薄與黏稠兩種岩漿及其造成的溫和與劇烈噴發,屬對比說明的組織方式。

talk.topic: Entropy and the second law of thermodynamics
talk.text: Let's explore one of the most fundamental ideas in physics: entropy and the second law of thermodynamics. Entropy is often described, loosely, as a measure of disorder, or more precisely, the number of ways a system's parts can be arranged.
