The second law states that in an isolated system, entropy tends to increase over time. In everyday terms, things naturally move from order toward disorder. Drop a glass and it shatters into pieces, but you will never see the pieces spontaneously leap back together into a glass. A hot cup of coffee cools to room temperature; it never warms itself back up by drawing heat from the cooler air. This one-way tendency is why physicists sometimes call the second law the arrow of time. It points out the direction in which time flows. Now, you might object that living things seem to create order. A plant builds an intricate structure from simple molecules. But there's no contradiction: the plant is not isolated. It takes in energy from sunlight, and in using that energy, it increases the total entropy of its surroundings.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How green plants grow using sunlight
Entropy and the second law of thermodynamics
The reasons glass is such a fragile material
The best way to keep a cup of coffee hot
questions.why: 講稿以熵與熱力學第二定律為核心,說明系統趨向無序及時間之箭,故主旨即為熵與熱力學第二定律。其他選項僅為文中例子。

questions.q: According to the professor, what does the second law say about an isolated system?
options: Its temperature always stays exactly constant
Its entropy tends to decrease over time
Its entropy tends to increase over time
Its total energy gradually disappears
questions.why: 講稿指出第二定律說孤立系統的熵傾向隨時間增加。選項提到熵減少或能量消失皆與定律相反。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention a growing plant?
options: To prove that the second law is sometimes false
To show that plants do not need any energy
To argue that living things are isolated systems
To address an apparent exception and show it is not a real contradiction
questions.why: 講稿以植物看似「創造秩序」為可能的反例,再說明植物並非孤立系統、會增加周遭總熵,故是為了回應表面矛盾並澄清並無牴觸。

questions.q: How does the professor make the idea of entropy easier to understand?
options: By giving familiar everyday examples such as a shattering glass
By presenting several detailed equations
By describing a single historical experiment
By comparing the views of several scientists
questions.why: 教授以打破的玻璃杯、變涼的咖啡等日常例子說明熵的概念,屬以熟悉實例輔助理解的組織方式。

talk.text: Today I want to tell you about the oldest light in the universe, known as the cosmic microwave background, or CMB. To understand it, we have to go back nearly fourteen billion years. In its first moments, the universe was incredibly hot and dense, a glowing fog of particles so thick that light could not travel freely. It kept bouncing off loose electrons. Then, as the universe expanded, it cooled. About three hundred eighty thousand years after the beginning, it became cool enough for electrons to join with nuclei and form neutral atoms. Suddenly the fog cleared, and light could stream across space for the first time. That ancient light is still traveling today. But because the universe has expanded so much since then, the light has been stretched into faint microwaves. Interestingly, its discovery in the 1960s was an accident: two engineers testing an antenna picked up a persistent hiss they couldn't get rid of, and it turned out to be this cosmic signal. The CMB is one of the strongest pieces of evidence supporting the Big Bang theory.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How radio antennas are built and tested
The reasons atoms are electrically neutral
The oldest light in the universe and what it tells us
The professional careers of two engineers
questions.why: 講稿主旨在介紹宇宙微波背景輻射這道最古老的光及其對大霹靂的證據意義,故答案為宇宙中最古老的光及其意涵。其他選項僅為細節。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happened about 380,000 years after the beginning?
options: The whole universe suddenly grew much hotter
The very first stars began to explode
Light stopped traveling through space entirely
Electrons joined nuclei, and the fog cleared so light could travel
questions.why: 講稿指出約三十八萬年後,宇宙冷卻到電子與原子核結合成中性原子,迷霧散去,光首度得以自由傳播。其他選項與此不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why the ancient light now appears as microwaves?
options: The expansion of the universe stretched the light
The light was already microwaves from the very start
Microwaves simply travel faster than visible light
The antenna itself converted the light into microwaves
questions.why: 講稿說因宇宙自那時起大幅膨脹,原本的光被拉長成微弱的微波,可推論宇宙膨脹使光波被拉伸。其他選項無依據。

questions.q: How does the professor mainly present the information?
options: By comparing several competing theories in detail
By tracing events from the early universe to the signal's discovery
By giving step-by-step instructions for building a telescope
By ranking different types of light by wavelength
questions.why: 教授依時間順序,從早期宇宙的高溫迷霧講到 1960 年代訊號被偶然發現,屬時間敘事的組織方式。

talk.text: How can scientists know what Earth's climate was like tens of thousands of years ago, long before anyone kept records? One remarkable answer lies frozen in the ice. In places like Antarctica and Greenland, snow falls year after year and never fully melts. Each year's snow gets buried and compressed by the layers above until it turns to solid ice. Scientists drill deep into these sheets and pull up long cylinders called ice cores. The deeper they drill, the further back in time they reach, because the deepest ice is the oldest. Now here's the clever part. As the snow was compressed, it trapped tiny bubbles of air. Each bubble is a genuine sample of the atmosphere from the year it was sealed. By analyzing these bubbles, researchers can measure how much carbon dioxide the air held thousands of years ago. The ice itself also records past temperatures through its chemistry. From cores like these, we've learned that carbon dioxide and temperature have risen and fallen together for hundreds of thousands of years, which helps us understand today's climate change.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To describe how snow forms over Antarctica
To explain the best way to drill through solid ice
To compare the ice of Greenland with that of Antarctica
To explain how ice cores reveal Earth's past climate
questions.why: 講稿主旨在說明冰芯如何保存古代空氣與溫度訊息,讓科學家重建古氣候,故答案為冰芯如何揭示地球過去的氣候。其他選項僅為過程細節。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is trapped inside the ice that is especially useful?
options: Tiny bubbles of ancient air
Small fragments of ancient plants
Fine grains of volcanic glass
Trapped drops of liquid water
questions.why: 講稿強調雪被壓實時封住了微小的空氣氣泡,是當年大氣的真實樣本,最為關鍵。其他選項並非文中所述。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the deepest layers of an ice core?
options: They contain the most recent air samples
They represent the oldest time periods
They have never actually been compressed
They hold no trapped air at all
questions.why: 講稿說鑽得越深、回溯的時間越久,最深的冰最古老,可推論冰芯最深層對應最古老的年代。其他選項與此矛盾。

questions.q: How does the professor begin the talk?
options: By carefully defining several scientific terms
By describing one specific drilling expedition
By posing a question about knowing the ancient climate
By listing the main gases found in the atmosphere
questions.why: 教授以「科學家如何得知數萬年前的氣候」這個問題開場,再引出冰芯的答案,屬以提問開場的組織方式。

talk.text: Today's topic is diffraction, the bending and spreading of waves when they pass around obstacles or through narrow openings. We usually picture light traveling in perfectly straight lines, and on large scales it does. But light is a wave, and when a wave meets an opening comparable in size to its wavelength, something interesting happens: it spreads out beyond the edges rather than casting a perfectly sharp shadow. You can see a version of this yourself. Look at a distant streetlight through the tiny gap between two fingers held close together, and you'll notice faint bands of light and dark. Those bands appear because waves passing through the gap overlap and interfere, reinforcing in some places and canceling in others. Diffraction has practical consequences. It sets a fundamental limit on the sharpness of any optical instrument. A telescope or microscope cannot resolve detail finer than a limit set by diffraction, no matter how perfectly its lenses are ground. This is one reason astronomers build ever-larger telescopes: a bigger opening reduces the blurring from diffraction and lets them see finer detail in the sky.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How waves bend and spread, and why this limits optical instruments
How a telescope lens is ground and polished
The reasons distant streetlights appear yellow
The strictly straight-line motion of light
questions.why: 講稿主旨在說明光的繞射(波的彎曲與擴散)及其對光學儀器解析度的限制,故答案為波如何彎曲擴散並限制光學儀器。其他選項僅為例子或反面。

questions.q: According to the professor, when does light noticeably spread out?
options: When it passes through a piece of colored glass
When it passes through an opening close to its wavelength in size
When it reflects off a smooth flat mirror
When it travels through completely empty space
questions.why: 講稿指出當波遇到與其波長相近大小的開口時,會明顯向邊緣外擴散。其他選項與繞射的條件不符。

questions.q: Why do astronomers build larger telescopes, according to the talk?
options: To collect and separate light of different colors
To make the telescopes easier to aim at targets
A larger opening reduces diffraction blurring and reveals finer detail
To avoid bending the incoming light at all
questions.why: 講稿說繞射會限制解析度,而更大的開口能減少繞射造成的模糊,故天文學家建更大望遠鏡以看到更細節,可由此推論。

questions.q: How does the professor support the explanation of diffraction?
options: By citing one famous historical experiment
By presenting a detailed mathematical equation
By comparing light with sound waves in depth
By suggesting a simple observation the listener can try
questions.why: 教授建議聽者用兩指間的細縫看遠處路燈,親自觀察明暗條紋,屬以可親身嘗試的簡易觀察來輔助說明。

talk.text: When a bright comet appears in our sky with its glowing tail, where does it actually come from? That's what I'd like to explore today. A comet is essentially a dirty snowball, a small body made of ice, dust, and rock. For most of its existence, a comet is a cold, dark lump drifting in the distant, frigid outer reaches of the solar system. Many are thought to originate from a vast, spherical shell of icy bodies surrounding the Sun far beyond the planets, called the Oort cloud. Occasionally, the gentle gravitational nudge of a passing star sends one of these bodies falling inward toward the Sun. As the comet approaches, sunlight heats its surface, and the ice turns directly to gas. This releases dust and vapor that form a glowing cloud around the comet and stream outward into a long tail. Here's a detail many people get wrong: a comet's tail does not trail behind its direction of motion. Instead, it's pushed by the solar wind and radiation, so it always points away from the Sun, even when the comet is heading back out.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How to photograph a comet's glowing tail
What comets are and where they come from
The gravity of stars that pass near the Sun
The reasons the Sun produces a solar wind
questions.why: 講稿主旨在說明彗星的組成、來源(歐特雲)與尾巴的成因,故答案為彗星是什麼以及來自何處。其他選項僅為片段細節。

questions.q: According to the professor, what causes a comet to form a glowing tail?
options: It collides directly with a passing star
It burns up like a lump of coal
Sunlight turns its ice to gas, releasing dust and vapor
It reflects light bouncing off the planets
questions.why: 講稿指出彗星接近太陽時,陽光使冰直接昇華為氣體,釋出塵埃與蒸氣形成發光的雲與尾巴。其他選項與成因不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a comet that is moving away from the Sun?
options: Its tail disappears almost immediately
Its tail trails directly behind its motion
It speeds up steadily as it leaves
Its tail still points away from the Sun
questions.why: 講稿說彗尾被太陽風與輻射推開,永遠指向背離太陽的方向,故彗星遠離太陽時,尾巴仍指向背離太陽的一方,可推論之。

questions.q: How does the professor begin the talk?
options: By raising a question about where comets come from
By carefully defining the term 'solar wind'
By describing a comet's impact on the Earth
By comparing two different famous comets
questions.why: 教授以「明亮的彗星究竟從何而來」這個問題開場,再逐步解答,屬以提問開場的組織方式。

talk.text: Earthquakes are destructive, but the waves they send through the Earth have also become one of our best tools for studying the planet's hidden interior. When an earthquake strikes, it releases energy as seismic waves, and there are two main types that travel through the Earth's body. The first are called P-waves, or primary waves. They push and pull the rock in the same direction they travel, like a spring being compressed, and crucially, they can pass through both solids and liquids. The second type are S-waves, or secondary waves. They shake the rock from side to side, and here's the key fact: they cannot travel through liquid at all. Now think about what that means. Seismometers around the world detected that S-waves fail to appear on the far side of the planet from a quake, as if something were blocking them. The best explanation is that the Earth's outer core is liquid. So without ever drilling down, scientists deduced the state of the Earth's core simply by tracking how these waves do, and do not, travel through the planet.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How to reduce earthquake damage to buildings
The physical difference between springs and rocks
How seismic waves reveal the Earth's interior
The regions where the largest earthquakes occur
questions.why: 講稿主旨在說明地震波(P 波與 S 波)如何幫助科學家推知地球內部結構,故答案為地震波如何揭示地球內部。其他選項與內容不符。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is a key property of S-waves?
options: They always travel faster than P-waves do
They pass through liquid quite easily
They push and pull along their direction of travel
They cannot travel through liquid
questions.why: 講稿明確指出 S 波(次波)完全無法穿過液體,這是關鍵事實。選項中把 S 波描述成能穿液或壓縮式運動的皆為 P 波性質或錯誤。

questions.q: Why do S-waves fail to appear on the far side of the planet from a quake?
options: The Earth's outer core is liquid and blocks them
They are absorbed entirely by the atmosphere
They travel far too slowly to ever arrive
Seismometers are unable to detect them
questions.why: 講稿說 S 波在地球另一側消失,最佳解釋是地球外核為液態而擋住了它們,可推論外核為液態阻擋了 S 波。

questions.q: How does the professor organize the explanation?
options: By narrating one single famous earthquake
By describing two wave types and drawing a conclusion from their behavior
By listing earthquakes from weakest to strongest
By giving instructions for operating a seismometer
questions.why: 教授先描述 P 波與 S 波兩種波的性質,再依其傳播行為推導出地核為液態的結論,屬先分述再據以推論的組織方式。

talk.text: Today I want to explain electromagnetic induction, the principle behind almost all the electricity we use. In the 1830s, Michael Faraday made a discovery that seems almost magical. He found that moving a magnet in and out of a coil of wire caused an electric current to flow in the wire, even though the magnet never touched it. The crucial point is motion, or more precisely, change. A magnet sitting still inside the coil produces nothing. It's only when the magnetic field through the coil changes that a current is generated. Move the magnet faster, and you get a stronger current. This simple principle is the heart of the electric generator. In a power plant, whether it's driven by falling water, steam from burning coal, or a nuclear reactor, the end goal is the same: to spin coils of wire near magnets, or magnets near coils. That constant changing of the magnetic field pushes electric current through the wires and out to our homes. So nearly every time you switch on a light, you're relying on Faraday's discovery from nearly two centuries ago.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: The personal life of Michael Faraday
How coal is burned inside a power plant
The reasons magnets attract pieces of iron
Electromagnetic induction and how it generates electricity
questions.why: 講稿以電磁感應為核心,說明變動的磁場如何產生電流並用於發電機,故主旨為電磁感應及其發電原理。其他選項僅為背景或次要提及。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is required to generate a current in the coil?
options: A changing magnetic field through the coil
A magnet resting perfectly still inside the coil
The magnet physically touching the wire
Heating the wire to a very high temperature
questions.why: 講稿強調關鍵在於「變化」——唯有穿過線圈的磁場改變時才會產生電流。靜止磁鐵、接觸或加熱皆非文中所述條件。

questions.q: What can be inferred about moving the magnet more quickly?
options: It stops the current from flowing entirely
It produces a stronger current
It reverses the poles of the magnet
It has no effect on the current at all
questions.why: 講稿說磁鐵移動越快,電流越強,可推論加快磁鐵運動會產生更強的電流。其他選項與此矛盾。

questions.q: How does the professor connect the topic to the listeners' lives?
options: By describing Faraday's laboratory in great detail
By comparing several different types of magnets
By pointing out that switching on a light relies on this principle
By listing the dates of major inventions in order
questions.why: 教授以「每次開燈都仰賴法拉第的發現」把主題連結到聽眾的日常生活,屬連結生活經驗的組織方式。

talk.text: Today I want to introduce one of the biggest unsolved puzzles in astronomy: dark matter. Here's the problem that led to it. When astronomers measured how fast stars orbit within galaxies, they found something baffling. The stars at the outer edges were moving far too quickly. Based on the gravity from all the visible matter, the stars, gas, and dust we can see, those outer stars should fly off into space. Yet they don't. The galaxies hold together as if there were far more mass present than we can actually see. There are two possible explanations. Either our understanding of gravity is incomplete, or there is a large amount of unseen matter providing the extra pull. Most astronomers favor the second idea. They propose that galaxies are embedded in vast halos of an invisible substance we call dark matter, which neither emits nor reflects light. We can't see it directly, but we detect its gravity. In fact, calculations suggest this mysterious material outweighs all the ordinary matter in the universe by roughly five to one. What it's actually made of, we still don't know.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Evidence for unseen matter that holds galaxies together
How ordinary stars produce light and heat
The long history of the telescope's invention
An argument that gravity does not really exist
questions.why: 講稿主旨在介紹暗物質:由星系旋轉異常推出有看不見的物質提供額外重力,故答案為維繫星系的不可見物質之證據。其他選項與內容不符。

questions.q: According to the professor, what did astronomers find puzzling?
options: Stars at galaxy edges had completely stopped moving
Outer stars were orbiting far too quickly for the visible mass
Galaxies turned out to be completely empty of gas
Light was seen bending around the edge of the Sun
questions.why: 講稿指出令人困惑的是星系外緣恆星運行過快,以可見物質的重力根本留不住它們。其他選項並非文中觀測結果。

questions.q: What can be inferred about dark matter?
options: It glows brightly when viewed through telescopes
It is simply a common type of ordinary star
It is detected through its gravitational effects, not directly
It makes up only a tiny fraction of the universe
questions.why: 講稿說暗物質不發光也不反光,無法直接看見,只能藉其重力偵測,且質量約為普通物質五倍,可推論其透過重力效應而非直接觀測被察覺。

questions.q: How does the professor present the topic?
options: By listing famous galaxies one after another
By narrating the discovery in strict chronological order
By giving step-by-step observing instructions
By presenting a puzzling observation and then possible explanations
questions.why: 教授先提出恆星運行過快這個令人困惑的觀測,再提出兩種可能解釋,屬「提出問題—提出解釋」的組織方式。

talk.text: Today we'll look at the rock cycle, the idea that rocks are not permanent but are constantly being transformed from one kind into another. Geologists group rocks into three families. Igneous rock forms when molten material cools and hardens, for example when lava freezes into solid stone. Sedimentary rock forms differently. Over long periods, wind and water break existing rock into tiny fragments, which settle in layers, and are gradually pressed and cemented together. Many sedimentary rocks, therefore, preserve fossils. The third family is metamorphic rock, which forms when existing rock is subjected to intense heat and pressure deep underground, changing its structure without fully melting it. Limestone, for instance, transforms into marble this way. Now, the key insight is that any of these rocks can become any other. A mountain of igneous rock can erode into sediment. That sediment can be buried, heated, and turned into metamorphic rock. And if it sinks deep enough to melt entirely, it may one day cool into igneous rock again. There is no beginning or end to this cycle; it has been turning for billions of years.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the talk?
options: To explain why fossils sometimes form inside marble
To describe how rocks change from one type into another
To argue that igneous rock is the oldest type
To list the mountains that are made of sediment
questions.why: 講稿主旨在說明岩石循環——三大類岩石如何互相轉變,故答案為描述岩石如何由一類轉變成另一類。其他選項與內容不符或僅為細節。

questions.q: According to the professor, how does metamorphic rock form?
options: When lava cools and hardens at the surface
When loose fragments settle in layers and are cemented
When existing rock is changed by intense heat and pressure
When fossils slowly accumulate over long periods
questions.why: 講稿指出變質岩是既有岩石在地下深處受強烈高溫高壓、結構改變但未完全熔化而成,如石灰岩變成大理岩。其他選項描述的是火成岩或沉積岩。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a single piece of rock over geologic time?
options: It always stays exactly the same type
It can only ever turn into sedimentary rock
It will eventually disappear completely
It may pass through more than one rock type
questions.why: 講稿強調任一類岩石都可能變成其他任一類,循環不斷,可推論一塊岩石在地質時間中可能歷經多種岩石類型。其他選項與循環概念矛盾。

questions.q: How does the professor mainly organize the talk?
options: By describing three rock types and then how they convert into one another
By telling the story of one single volcano
By ranking rocks according to their hardness
By comparing rocks on Earth with rocks on the Moon
questions.why: 教授先分別介紹火成岩、沉積岩、變質岩三類,再說明它們如何彼此轉換,屬先分類再說明循環的組織方式。

talk.text: Let me introduce one of the strangest ideas in all of physics: wave-particle duality. For a long time, scientists argued about the nature of light. Was it a stream of tiny particles, or a wave rippling through space? Both sides had strong evidence. Light bends and spreads like a wave, yet it also delivers its energy in discrete packets, as if it were made of particles. The resolution, which emerged in the early twentieth century, was startling: light is somehow both, and so is everything else. Consider the famous double-slit experiment. When particles of light, or even single electrons, are fired one at a time at a barrier with two narrow slits, they build up a striped pattern on the screen behind, exactly the pattern you'd expect from interfering waves. Yet each one arrives as a single dot, like a particle. Stranger still, if you set up a detector to watch which slit each particle goes through, the wave pattern vanishes. The act of measuring changes the outcome. This puzzling behavior lies at the very foundation of quantum mechanics, and physicists are still debating what it truly means.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How to build a working double-slit apparatus
An argument that light is purely a wave
The idea that light and matter behave as both waves and particles
The history of arguments among various scientists
questions.why: 講稿以波粒二象性為核心,說明光與物質同時具有波與粒子的性質,故主旨為光與物質兼具波與粒子行為的概念。其他選項與內容不符或僅為片段。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens in the double-slit experiment when particles are fired one at a time?
options: They pass through only one slit and then stop
They merge together into a single beam of light
They fall straight down without forming any pattern
They build up a striped interference pattern on the screen
questions.why: 講稿指出即使一次只發射一個光粒子或電子,它們仍在螢幕上累積成條紋干涉圖樣,如同波般。其他選項與實驗結果不符。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the fact that watching which slit is used makes the pattern vanish?
options: The act of measurement can affect the outcome
The detector used must simply be broken
Electrons are far too small to ever detect
The two slits close automatically on their own
questions.why: 講稿說一旦設偵測器觀察粒子走哪條縫,波的圖樣就消失,顯示測量行為本身會改變結果,可推論觀測會影響結果。其他選項無依據。

questions.q: How does the professor introduce the topic?
options: By giving several detailed mathematical formulas
By presenting a historical debate over the nature of light
By describing a range of modern electronic devices
By comparing light directly with ordinary sound waves
questions.why: 教授以科學家長久以來爭論光是粒子還是波這段歷史辯論開場,再引出波粒二象性,屬以歷史爭論切入的組織方式。

talk.topic: 行為經濟 · 錨定效應 (anchoring effect)
talk.text: Alright, let's talk about one of the most stubborn quirks in human decision-making: the anchoring effect. Here's the basic idea. When people estimate a value, they lean heavily on the first number they happen to see, even when that number is completely irrelevant. Let me give you a classic example. In one study, researchers spun a wheel of fortune that landed on either ten or sixty-five. Then they asked people what percentage of countries in the United Nations were African. Now, the wheel had nothing to do with geography, right? Yet people who saw sixty-five guessed far higher than those who saw ten. That first number became an anchor, and their estimates drifted toward it. So why does this matter for business? Think about pricing. When a store lists an item at an original price of two hundred dollars and then marks it down to ninety-nine, that two hundred is the anchor. Suddenly ninety-nine feels like a bargain, even if the item was never really worth two hundred. The lesson here is that our judgments are rarely built from scratch. We adjust from whatever starting point is handed to us, and often we don't adjust nearly enough.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How stores decide the original price of their products.
How an initial number influences people's later judgments.
Why the United Nations tracks its member countries.
How consumers calculate the true value of a product.
questions.why: 全文主旨是最初看到的數字如何影響人們後續的判斷,即錨定效應。

questions.q: In the study the professor describes, what did the wheel of fortune determine?
options: Which country each participant had to describe.
How much money participants would be paid.
The number participants saw before making an estimate.
The percentage of African countries in the UN.
questions.why: 轉盤決定了受試者在估計前先看到的數字(十或六十五),此數字成為錨點。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a shopper who sees a marked-down price?
options: They will always refuse to buy the item.
They usually know the item's true manufacturing cost.
They ignore the original price entirely.
They may judge the deal relative to the original price.
questions.why: 由文中定價例子可推論,消費者會以原價為錨,覺得折扣後的價格划算。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the wheel of fortune experiment?
options: To demonstrate that even an irrelevant number can anchor estimates.
To prove that most people know world geography well.
To explain how casinos design their games.
To show that the United Nations is hard to study.
questions.why: 提到轉盤實驗是為了說明即使是毫不相關的數字也會成為估計的錨點。

talk.topic: 供需 · 需求價格彈性 (price elasticity of demand)
talk.text: Okay, today I want to introduce a concept economists use constantly: price elasticity of demand. In plain terms, elasticity measures how sensitive buyers are to a change in price. If a small price increase causes people to buy a lot less, we say demand is elastic. If they keep buying about the same amount no matter what, demand is inelastic. Let me contrast two products. Take a fancy brand of potato chips. If the price jumps, shoppers simply switch to another brand, so demand is elastic. Now compare that with insulin, a medicine that diabetics need to survive. Even if the price doubles, people will still buy it, because there's no substitute and they can't do without it. That's classic inelastic demand. So what determines elasticity? Two big factors stand out. First, whether close substitutes exist. The more alternatives, the more elastic. Second, whether the good is a necessity or a luxury. Necessities tend to be inelastic. This idea has real consequences. A company selling an inelastic product can raise prices and actually earn more revenue, while the same move could be disastrous for a firm whose customers can easily walk away.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How responsive buyers are to changes in price.
Why insulin is so expensive to produce.
How companies advertise potato chips.
Why substitutes should always be banned.
questions.why: 全文主旨是需求價格彈性,即買家對價格變動的敏感程度。

questions.q: According to the professor, why is demand for insulin inelastic?
options: Because it is cheaper than potato chips.
Because many brands compete to sell it.
Because diabetics rarely need it.
Because it is a necessity with no close substitute.
questions.why: 胰島素是必需品且沒有替代品,因此需求缺乏彈性。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a product with many substitutes?
options: Its demand will be highly inelastic.
Its demand will tend to be elastic.
It must be a medical necessity.
Its price can never change.
questions.why: 文中指出替代品越多,需求越有彈性,故有許多替代品的產品需求偏向彈性。

questions.q: Why does the professor compare potato chips and insulin?
options: To argue that snacks are healthier than medicine.
To show that both products have the same price.
To illustrate the difference between elastic and inelastic demand.
To explain how insulin is manufactured.
questions.why: 以洋芋片與胰島素對比,是為了說明彈性與缺乏彈性需求的差別。

talk.topic: 行銷 · 市場區隔 (market segmentation)
talk.text: So let's move on to a foundational idea in marketing: market segmentation. The core insight is simple. A single market is almost never one uniform mass of identical customers. Instead, it's made up of distinct groups, each with different needs, tastes, and budgets. Segmentation is the process of dividing that broad market into these smaller, more similar groups so a company can serve each one better. Marketers typically slice a market along a few dimensions. One is demographics, things like age, income, or family size. Another is geography, since a customer in a cold northern city wants different products than one near the equator. A third is behavior, such as how often someone uses a product or how loyal they are. Consider a car manufacturer. Rather than building one vehicle for everyone, it offers a fuel-efficient compact for young city drivers, a spacious minivan for families, and a luxury sedan for wealthy buyers. Each model targets a specific segment. Why bother? Because a message crafted for a particular group almost always lands harder than a generic one aimed at nobody in particular. Segmentation lets a firm spend its limited marketing budget where it will actually resonate.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How car manufacturers design their engines.
Why marketing budgets keep increasing.
How companies divide a market into distinct customer groups.
Why every customer wants the same product.
questions.why: 全文主旨是市場區隔,即公司如何把市場分成不同的顧客群。

questions.q: Which of the following is mentioned as a way to segment a market?
options: By demographic factors such as age and income.
By the color of the product packaging.
By the number of competitors in the industry.
By the year the company was founded.
questions.why: 教授提到可依人口統計特徵如年齡與收入來區隔市場。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a generic advertisement aimed at everyone?
options: It is always cheaper to produce than a targeted one.
It will reach wealthy buyers most effectively.
It is required by most governments.
It is likely to be less effective than a targeted message.
questions.why: 文中指出針對特定群體的訊息比泛泛而談的更有效,故通用廣告效果可能較差。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe the car manufacturer's different models?
options: To argue that minivans are the best type of car.
To give a concrete example of serving different segments.
To explain how car engines are built.
To show that luxury sedans never sell well.
questions.why: 舉汽車廠不同車型為例,是為了具體說明如何服務不同的市場區隔。

talk.topic: 創業 · 精實創業與 MVP (lean startup)
talk.text: Alright, let's dig into a philosophy that reshaped how new companies get built: the lean startup. For a long time, the standard advice was to spend months, even years, perfecting a product in secret, then launch it with a big splash. The trouble is, founders often poured everything into building something nobody actually wanted. The lean approach flips that script. Instead of a finished product, you start with what's called a minimum viable product, or MVP. This is the simplest version of your idea that still delivers some value, something you can put in front of real customers quickly. The goal isn't to impress. It's to learn. You release the MVP, watch how people respond, gather feedback, and then adjust. This cycle, build, measure, learn, repeats over and over. Say you dream of a meal-delivery app. Rather than coding for a year, you might start by taking orders through a simple web form and delivering the food yourself. Crude, yes, but you'll discover fast whether anyone wants the service. The underlying wisdom is that assumptions are dangerous. A founder's job is to test them cheaply and early, before sinking a fortune into something the market may reject.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: Why meal-delivery apps usually fail.
How to keep a new product secret until launch.
Why founders should avoid customer feedback.
How the lean startup tests ideas quickly and cheaply.
questions.why: 全文主旨是精實創業,即如何快速且低成本地測試創業構想。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is the purpose of a minimum viable product?
options: To impress investors with a polished design.
To learn from real customers as quickly as possible.
To earn as much profit as possible on launch day.
To keep the idea hidden from competitors.
questions.why: 教授說 MVP 的目的是盡快從真實顧客身上學習,而非炫技。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the traditional approach to launching a product?
options: It risked building something customers did not want.
It always produced higher-quality products.
It relied heavily on early customer feedback.
It was cheaper than the lean approach.
questions.why: 傳統做法花大量時間閉門開發,風險是做出顧客不想要的產品。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the meal-delivery app?
options: To prove that web forms are outdated technology.
To warn that food delivery is unprofitable.
To illustrate how an MVP can be simple and manual.
To describe how to write computer code.
questions.why: 以外送 app 為例,是為了說明 MVP 可以是簡單甚至手動的版本。

talk.topic: 勞動市場 · 人力資本與工資 (human capital)
talk.text: Today I want to explore why some workers earn far more than others. Economists often turn to a concept called human capital. Just as a factory invests in machines, individuals invest in themselves, through education, training, and on-the-job experience. Human capital refers to the skills and knowledge a worker carries, which make that worker more productive. And here's the key link: in a competitive labor market, workers tend to be paid roughly according to how productive they are. So the more human capital you accumulate, the more you can typically command in wages. This helps explain the well-documented gap between, say, a college graduate and someone with only a high school diploma. On average, the graduate earns considerably more over a lifetime. Part of that reflects the additional skills gained during those years of study. But notice a subtle point. Not all of the wage gap is necessarily about skill. Some economists argue a degree also acts as a signal to employers, a way of showing you're capable and disciplined, even apart from what you actually learned. Either way, investing in yourself tends to pay off, though, like any investment, it carries costs and some risk.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why investing in skills tends to raise a worker's wages.
Why factories prefer machines to workers.
How colleges decide which students to admit.
Why all workers earn the same amount.
questions.why: 全文主旨是人力資本,說明投資技能為何通常能提高工資。

questions.q: According to the professor, what does human capital refer to?
options: The money a company spends on new machines.
The number of workers a factory employs.
The skills and knowledge that make a worker more productive.
The wages paid to high school graduates.
questions.why: 人力資本指的是讓工作者更有生產力的技能與知識。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a worker who gains more training?
options: The worker will earn lower wages over time.
The worker is likely to become more productive.
The worker will be replaced by a machine.
The worker no longer needs a college degree.
questions.why: 文中指出人力資本使工作者更有生產力,故接受更多訓練者可能生產力提高。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the idea of a degree as a signal?
options: To prove that education teaches no useful skills.
To argue that wages have nothing to do with skill.
To explain how factories buy machines.
To note that not all of the wage gap comes from skill alone.
questions.why: 提到學位是一種訊號,是為了指出工資差距未必全然來自技能本身。

talk.topic: 行為經濟 · 損失趨避 (loss aversion)
talk.text: Let's continue with another powerful force in behavioral economics: loss aversion. The finding, first documented by Kahneman and Tversky, is that losses hurt more than equivalent gains feel good. Roughly speaking, the pain of losing twenty dollars is about twice as intense as the pleasure of finding twenty dollars. We are not neutral about gains and losses. We are wired to fear losing. Watch how this shows up in everyday choices. Imagine I offer you a coin flip: heads you win a hundred and ten dollars, tails you lose a hundred. Mathematically, that's a good bet. Yet most people refuse, because the possible loss looms far larger than the slightly bigger possible gain. Businesses exploit this constantly. Consider free trials. Once you've used a product for a month, giving it up feels like a loss, so you keep paying to avoid that sting. Or think about how a warranty is sold by emphasizing what you might lose, not what you'd gain. The broader takeaway is that we don't evaluate outcomes in the cold, rational way older economic models assumed. Instead, we judge them against a reference point, and losing ground from that point is what we dread most.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How to win money by flipping coins.
Why free trials are always a bad deal.
Why people fear losses more than they value equivalent gains.
How economists calculate the odds of a bet.
questions.why: 全文主旨是損失趨避,即人們對損失的恐懼大於對等額獲利的喜悅。

questions.q: According to the professor, how does the pain of a loss compare to the pleasure of a gain?
options: It is exactly equal to it.
It is much weaker than it.
It lasts a shorter time than it.
It is roughly twice as intense as it.
questions.why: 教授說損失的痛苦大約是同等獲利喜悅的兩倍強烈。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why people keep paying after a free trial?
options: They want to avoid the feeling of losing the product.
They enjoy spending money for its own sake.
They have forgotten they signed up for the trial.
They believe the product is completely free.
questions.why: 免費試用後停用會感覺像失去,人們為避免這種損失感而繼續付費。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe the coin-flip offer?
options: To teach students how to gamble responsibly.
To show that people reject bets when losses loom larger.
To prove that the bet is mathematically unfair.
To explain how casinos calculate their profits.
questions.why: 以擲硬幣的賭注為例,是為了說明當損失感更大時人們會拒絕下注。

talk.topic: 供需 · 價格上限與短缺 (price ceilings)
talk.text: Okay, let's examine what happens when a government tries to hold a price down by law. This is called a price ceiling, a legal maximum that sellers are not allowed to exceed. The intention is usually generous, to keep something like housing or fuel affordable. But basic supply and demand tells us a ceiling set below the natural market price creates a problem: a shortage. Here's the logic. When the price is forced low, two things happen at once. Buyers want to purchase more at that bargain price, so the quantity demanded rises. Meanwhile, sellers find it less profitable to supply, so the quantity offered falls. Demand outstrips supply, and suddenly there isn't enough to go around. Rent control is the textbook case. Cap rents below market level, and more people want apartments while landlords build fewer of them. The result is long waiting lists and units that never come up for rent. Shortages also breed side effects, like black markets or sellers cutting quality since buyers are lined up anyway. The lesson isn't that helping people is wrong. It's that prices carry information, and when you freeze that signal, the market finds other, often messier, ways to ration what's scarce.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why governments should never regulate markets.
How a price ceiling below the market price causes a shortage.
How landlords set the rent for their apartments.
Why black markets are good for consumers.
questions.why: 全文主旨是低於市場價的價格上限如何造成短缺。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens to quantity demanded when a price is forced below the market level?
options: It rises, because buyers want more at the low price.
It falls, because buyers lose interest.
It stays exactly the same.
It disappears entirely.
questions.why: 價格被壓低時,買家在低價下想買更多,需求量上升。

questions.q: What can be inferred about landlords under strict rent control?
options: They will build many new apartments.
They will lower rents even further voluntarily.
They have less incentive to supply rental housing.
They will always improve the quality of units.
questions.why: 租金被限制在低於市場水準,房東供給住房的誘因降低。

questions.q: How does the professor develop the explanation of price ceilings?
options: By comparing several different countries' laws.
By describing the history of rent control over time.
By interviewing tenants about their experiences.
By explaining the cause and then giving rent control as an example.
questions.why: 教授先說明價格上限造成短缺的原理,再以租金管制為例,屬因果加舉例的結構。

talk.topic: 行銷 · 品牌忠誠 (brand loyalty)
talk.text: Let's turn now to something every company dreams of building: brand loyalty. This is the tendency of customers to keep choosing the same brand again and again, even when competitors offer similar products, sometimes at lower prices. A loyal customer doesn't shop around much. They reach for the familiar name almost automatically. Why does this matter so much? Two reasons stand out. First, keeping an existing customer is far cheaper than winning a new one. Attracting a stranger requires expensive advertising, while a loyal buyer returns on their own. Second, loyal customers are less sensitive to price. They'll often pay a premium rather than switch, which protects the company's profit margins. So how do firms cultivate loyalty? Consistency is one pillar, delivering the same quality every single time so customers know exactly what to expect. Emotional connection is another, which is why brands invest so heavily in storytelling and identity. Loyalty programs, those points and rewards cards, add a practical layer by making it costly to leave. But here's a caution I want you to remember. Loyalty is fragile. Years of goodwill can evaporate after a single scandal or a serious drop in quality. Trust is slow to build and quick to lose.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How to design a rewards card program.
Why advertising is a waste of money.
How companies set the prices of their products.
Why brand loyalty is valuable and how firms build it.
questions.why: 全文主旨是品牌忠誠的價值以及公司如何培養它。

questions.q: According to the professor, why are loyal customers valuable regarding price?
options: They always demand the lowest possible price.
They refuse to buy anything on sale.
They are less sensitive to price and may pay a premium.
They only buy during loyalty program promotions.
questions.why: 忠誠顧客對價格較不敏感,常願意支付較高價格,保護了公司利潤。

questions.q: What can be inferred about acquiring a brand-new customer?
options: It is usually cheaper than keeping an existing one.
It typically costs more than retaining a loyal one.
It requires no advertising at all.
It guarantees long-term loyalty.
questions.why: 文中指出留住舊顧客比爭取新顧客便宜,故爭取新顧客成本較高。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention scandals at the end of the talk?
options: To warn that hard-won loyalty can be lost quickly.
To explain how loyalty programs earn points.
To prove that all brands eventually fail.
To describe how advertising campaigns are made.
questions.why: 結尾提到醜聞,是為了警示辛苦建立的忠誠可能瞬間流失。

talk.topic: 創業 · 網路效應與平台 (network effects)
talk.text: Let's talk about why certain companies grow so explosively and become almost impossible to unseat. The secret is often something called a network effect. A product has a network effect when each new user makes it more valuable to every other user. Think about a telephone. A single phone is useless. Two phones let you call one person. But a network of millions makes each phone enormously valuable. The value grows as the network grows. This is the engine behind many platform businesses. Consider a marketplace that connects buyers and sellers. The more sellers who list goods, the more buyers show up, and the more buyers arrive, the more sellers want to join. Each side pulls in the other, creating a self-reinforcing loop. For an entrepreneur, network effects are a double-edged sword. Early on, they're brutal. When your platform is empty, why would anyone join? This is the classic chicken-and-egg problem. But once you cross a critical mass, the same dynamic becomes a powerful moat. Competitors can't easily lure users away, because the value lives in the crowd, not just the software. That's why so many startups burn cash early, racing to be the first to reach that tipping point.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How network effects make a product more valuable as users grow.
Why telephones are no longer useful.
How marketplaces set prices for sellers.
Why startups should avoid spending money.
questions.why: 全文主旨是網路效應如何隨使用者增加而提升產品價值。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is the 'chicken-and-egg problem' for a new platform?
options: Deciding whether to sell chickens or eggs.
Attracting users when the platform is still empty.
Choosing which software language to use.
Setting the correct price for early adopters.
questions.why: 雞生蛋問題指的是平台一開始空無一人時,難以吸引使用者加入。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a platform that has passed critical mass?
options: It will quickly lose all of its users.
It no longer provides any value to users.
It must lower its prices to survive.
It becomes harder for competitors to displace.
questions.why: 達到臨界規模後,價值來自龐大用戶群,競爭者難以搶走使用者。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe a single telephone?
options: To argue that telephones are outdated technology.
To explain how phone companies earn profit.
To illustrate that value grows as the network grows.
To compare phones with modern software.
questions.why: 以單支電話為例,是為了說明價值會隨網路規模擴大而增加。

talk.topic: 勞動市場 · 最低工資 (minimum wage)
talk.text: Today let's tackle one of the most debated topics in labor economics: the minimum wage. A minimum wage is a legal floor on pay; employers cannot pay workers less than that amount per hour. The goal is to ensure that people who work full-time can afford a decent living. Now, the standard textbook model makes a stark prediction. If you set a wage floor above what the market would naturally pay, employers will hire fewer workers, because labor has become more expensive. In that view, a higher minimum wage helps those who keep their jobs but throws others out of work. For years, that was the settled story. But here's where it gets interesting. Some influential real-world studies, comparing neighboring regions with different minimum wages, found little or no drop in employment after modest increases. Why might that be? One explanation is that employers sometimes have wage-setting power, so a small floor can raise pay without killing jobs. Another is that better-paid workers quit less, cutting turnover costs. The honest conclusion is that the effect depends on how high the floor is set and on local conditions. A modest increase and a drastic one are simply not the same policy.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why all businesses oppose paying their workers.
How workers decide which jobs to accept.
The debated effects of the minimum wage on employment.
Why textbooks are always wrong about economics.
questions.why: 全文主旨是最低工資對就業影響的爭論。

questions.q: According to the standard textbook model, what happens when a wage floor is set above the market wage?
options: Employers hire many more workers.
Employers tend to hire fewer workers.
Wages fall below the legal minimum.
Turnover costs rise sharply for workers.
questions.why: 標準教科書模型預測,工資下限高於市場工資時,雇主會雇用較少工人。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the studies of neighboring regions?
options: A modest minimum-wage increase need not cause large job losses.
Minimum wages always eliminate most jobs.
Employers never have any wage-setting power.
Higher pay always increases worker turnover.
questions.why: 比較相鄰地區的研究顯示,溫和調高最低工資未必造成大量失業。

questions.q: How does the professor structure the discussion of the minimum wage?
options: By listing the wages of many different countries.
By interviewing workers who lost their jobs.
By describing the history of labor unions.
By presenting the textbook prediction, then contrasting it with real-world findings.
questions.why: 教授先提出教科書預測,再以真實研究結果對照,屬對比的結構。

talk.topic: 行為經濟 · 推力與預設選項 (nudges & defaults)
talk.text: Let's explore a gentle but surprisingly powerful tool from behavioral economics: the nudge. A nudge is a small change in how choices are presented that steers people toward a particular decision, without forbidding any option or changing the incentives. You're still completely free to choose. The environment is just arranged to make one path easier. The most famous nudge involves default options. Whatever happens if you do nothing tends to stick, because people are prone to inertia. Consider retirement savings. When employees have to actively sign up for a savings plan, many never get around to it. But flip the default, so they're automatically enrolled unless they opt out, and participation jumps dramatically. Nobody lost any freedom; the lazy path simply changed. The same principle appears in organ donation rates, which are far higher in countries where you're a donor by default. Now, nudges raise ethical questions. Who decides which direction to steer people? Critics worry about manipulation, even if it's well-intentioned. Defenders reply that choices always have to be presented somehow, so you might as well arrange them to help people reach the goals they'd choose for themselves anyway. The debate is really about the responsibility that comes with designing choices.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: Why people should be forced to save for retirement.
How to remove all choices from consumers.
Why organ donation should be made illegal.
How small changes in how choices are presented influence decisions.
questions.why: 全文主旨是推力,即呈現選項方式的微小改變如何影響決策。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens to retirement plan participation when enrollment is automatic?
options: It increases dramatically.
It falls to almost zero.
It stays exactly the same.
It becomes illegal.
questions.why: 改為自動加入(需主動退出)後,退休儲蓄計畫的參與率大幅上升。

questions.q: What can be inferred about people's response to default options?
options: They usually reject whatever the default is.
They carefully analyze every option before deciding.
They often stick with whatever requires no action.
They prefer options that cost the most money.
questions.why: 由慣性可推論,人們常會維持不需採取行動的預設選項。

questions.q: Why does the professor raise ethical questions near the end?
options: To prove that nudges never work in practice.
To acknowledge concerns about who decides the direction of a nudge.
To explain how retirement accounts earn interest.
To argue that all defaults should be banned.
questions.why: 結尾提出倫理問題,是為了承認關於由誰決定推力方向的疑慮。

talk.topic: 供需 · 需求移動與沿線移動 (demand shifts)
talk.text: Alright, I want to clear up a confusion that trips up almost every beginning economics student: the difference between a movement along the demand curve and a shift of the entire curve. These sound similar, but they mean very different things. Let's start with a movement along the curve. This happens when the price of the good itself changes, and nothing else. If coffee gets more expensive, people buy a little less coffee. We slide up along the same curve. The relationship hasn't changed; we've just moved to a different point on it. A shift of the whole curve is different. Here, something other than the good's own price changes, and the entire curve moves left or right. Suppose a new study announces that coffee is wonderful for your health. Now, at every single price, people want more coffee than before. The whole curve jumps to the right. Other shifters include income, the price of related goods like tea, and even expectations about the future. So here's the rule to burn into memory. A change in the good's own price moves you along the curve. A change in anything else shifts the curve itself. Mixing these up leads to endless analytical errors.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why coffee is bad for your health.
The difference between moving along a demand curve and shifting it.
How to calculate the exact price of coffee.
Why students should not study economics.
questions.why: 全文主旨是沿需求曲線移動與整條曲線移動之間的差別。

questions.q: According to the professor, what causes a movement along the demand curve?
options: A change in consumers' income.
A new health study about the product.
A change in the price of a related good.
A change in the price of the good itself.
questions.why: 沿曲線移動是由該商品本身價格的變動所引起。

questions.q: What can be inferred if a study makes coffee more popular at every price?
options: The entire demand curve shifts to the right.
Consumers simply move up along the same curve.
The price of coffee must have risen.
Demand for coffee disappears completely.
questions.why: 在每個價格下都想買更多,代表整條需求曲線向右移動。

questions.q: Why does the professor use coffee throughout the talk?
options: To argue that coffee should be more expensive.
To describe how coffee is grown and sold.
To use one consistent example to contrast the two concepts.
To prove that health studies are unreliable.
questions.why: 全程以咖啡為單一例子,是為了對比沿曲線移動與曲線移動兩個概念。

talk.topic: 行銷 · 誘餌效應定價 (decoy effect)
talk.text: Let's look at a clever pricing trick marketers use to steer your choice: the decoy effect. It works like this. When people face two options, they often struggle to decide. But add a third, carefully designed option, the decoy, and suddenly one of the original choices looks obviously better. The decoy isn't meant to sell. It's there to make something else more attractive. A famous example comes from a magazine's subscription offer. They listed a web-only subscription for fifty-nine dollars and a combined print-and-web subscription for one hundred and twenty-five. When those were the only choices, most people picked the cheaper web-only option. Then the magazine added a third option: print-only, also priced at one hundred and twenty-five dollars, the same as the combined deal. Now the combined print-and-web looked like a steal, because for the same price you got both formats instead of just print. Sales of the expensive combined option shot up. Notice what happened. The print-only decoy was almost never chosen itself. Its only job was to make the combined package look like a bargain by comparison. This reveals something deep about us. We rarely judge value in absolute terms. We judge it relative to whatever alternatives sit beside it.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How adding a third option can steer people toward a pricier choice.
Why magazines are going out of business.
How to calculate the cost of printing.
Why people always choose the cheapest option.
questions.why: 全文主旨是誘餌效應,即加入第三個選項如何引導人們選擇較貴的方案。

questions.q: According to the professor, what was the price of the print-only decoy option?
options: Fifty-nine dollars.
Free of charge.
The same as the combined print-and-web option.
Twice the price of the web-only option.
questions.why: 只印刷的誘餌選項定價與印刷加網路的組合方案相同。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the print-only option?
options: It was the most popular choice among readers.
It was cheaper than the web-only option.
It was designed to be the best value.
It existed mainly to make another option look better.
questions.why: 只印刷選項幾乎沒人選,其存在主要是為了襯托組合方案更划算。

questions.q: Why does the professor describe the magazine's subscription offer?
options: To argue that print magazines are superior to websites.
To give a real example of how a decoy changes choices.
To explain how magazines are printed.
To show that subscriptions are a waste of money.
questions.why: 以雜誌訂閱方案為例,是為了具體說明誘餌如何改變人們的選擇。

talk.topic: 創業 · 自力創業與創投 (bootstrapping vs VC)
talk.text: Today let's compare two very different ways to fund a new company: bootstrapping and venture capital. Bootstrapping means growing the business using your own savings and, crucially, the revenue the company itself generates. You reinvest every dollar you earn. The great advantage is control. You own the whole thing and answer to no outside investors. The downside is that growth can be painfully slow, and you're risking your personal money. Venture capital takes the opposite path. Here, investors hand you a large sum of cash in exchange for a slice of ownership, a share of equity. This lets you grow fast, hiring aggressively and spending to capture the market before rivals do. But that speed comes at a price. You give up part of your company, and investors will expect rapid growth and a big payoff, which brings pressure and a loss of some control. So which is better? It really depends on the business. A software startup racing against competitors in a winner-take-all market may need venture money to move fast enough. A steady local business with modest ambitions might be far happier bootstrapped. The key question a founder must ask is simple: how fast do I truly need to grow, and what am I willing to give up to get there?

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why software startups always fail.
How investors calculate a company's value.
Comparing two ways to fund a new company.
Why founders should never accept outside money.
questions.why: 全文主旨是比較自力創業與創投兩種為新公司募資的方式。

questions.q: According to the professor, what does bootstrapping rely on?
options: The founder's savings and the company's own revenue.
Large sums of cash from outside investors.
Government grants and subsidies.
Loans from competing companies.
questions.why: 自力創業依靠創業者自己的積蓄以及公司本身產生的營收。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a founder who takes venture capital?
options: They keep full ownership of the company.
They give up some ownership and control.
They no longer need the business to grow.
They avoid all financial pressure.
questions.why: 接受創投的創業者以股權換取資金,因此放棄部分所有權與控制權。

questions.q: How does the professor conclude the talk?
options: By insisting that venture capital is always the best choice.
By listing the names of famous investors.
By explaining how to write a business plan.
By suggesting the choice depends on how fast the business must grow.
questions.why: 教授以「該多快成長、願意放棄什麼」作結,指出選擇取決於企業需要成長的速度。

talk.topic: 勞動市場 · 訊號理論 (job market signaling)
talk.text: Let's examine a puzzle in the economics of education. We usually assume school raises wages because it teaches useful skills, building human capital. But an economist named Michael Spence offered a strikingly different explanation, called signaling. Here's the setup. Imagine employers who want to hire capable, hardworking people, but they can't directly observe those qualities in a job applicant. Talent is hidden. So how do they decide whom to hire? Spence's insight is that a credential, like a college degree, can serve as a signal. The crucial condition is this: the signal must be harder for less-capable people to obtain than for capable ones. Earning a demanding degree is tough. If naturally able students find it much easier to complete than others, then holding the degree reliably signals ability, even if the coursework itself taught nothing directly useful for the job. Employers rationally pay more for degree-holders, not necessarily for what they learned, but for what the degree reveals about them. Now, this has an unsettling implication. If education is partly signaling, then some of the money society spends on schooling isn't building skills at all. It's just helping people prove abilities they already had. Most economists think the truth blends both stories.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: Why colleges should lower their tuition.
How employers directly measure applicants' talent.
Why education never teaches useful skills.
The idea that a degree can serve as a signal of ability.
questions.why: 全文主旨是訊號理論,即學位可作為能力的訊號。

questions.q: According to Spence, what condition must a signal meet to be reliable?
options: It must be equally easy for everyone to obtain.
It must be harder for less-capable people to obtain.
It must be given away for free by employers.
It must teach directly job-relevant skills.
questions.why: 史賓斯指出,訊號必須是能力較差者較難取得,才能可靠地反映能力。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why employers pay more for degree-holders under signaling theory?
options: Because the coursework always teaches job skills.
Because degrees are required by law.
Because the degree reveals hidden ability.
Because degree-holders demand lower wages.
questions.why: 依訊號理論,雇主付較高薪是因為學位揭示了求職者隱藏的能力。

questions.q: How does the professor introduce the signaling theory?
options: By contrasting it with the human capital explanation.
By describing the history of universities.
By listing the salaries of various professions.
By interviewing several employers directly.
questions.why: 教授藉由與人力資本解釋作對比,來引入訊號理論。

talk.topic: 行為經濟 · 現時偏誤 (present bias)
talk.text: Let's discuss why so many of us make plans we fail to keep: present bias. The idea is that people place far too much weight on immediate rewards compared with future ones, even when the future payoff is clearly larger. We are impatient in a very particular way. Here's a classic demonstration. Ask people whether they'd rather have a hundred dollars today or a hundred and ten a week from now. Many grab the hundred today, unwilling to wait even for the extra ten. But now push both options a year into the future. Would you prefer a hundred dollars in fifty-two weeks, or a hundred and ten in fifty-three weeks? Suddenly almost everyone waits the extra week. Notice it's the same one-week trade-off in both cases. What changed is only whether the reward is available right now. That's the signature of present bias: our patience collapses when temptation is immediate. This explains a lot of everyday struggles, dieting, saving money, exercising. We genuinely plan to start tomorrow, but when tomorrow arrives and becomes today, the immediate cost feels overwhelming again. Understanding this helps design solutions, like commitment devices that lock in our future selves before temptation strikes.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why people should never save money.
Why people overvalue immediate rewards over future ones.
How banks calculate interest rates.
Why waiting a week is always the best choice.
questions.why: 全文主旨是現時偏誤,即人們過度重視眼前的報酬勝過未來的報酬。

questions.q: In the example, what changes when both options are pushed a year into the future?
options: The amounts of money become much larger.
People become even more impatient.
Almost everyone is willing to wait the extra week.
The one-week gap disappears entirely.
questions.why: 當兩個選項都推遲到一年後,幾乎每個人都願意多等一週。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why people struggle to start a diet 'tomorrow'?
options: When tomorrow becomes today, the immediate cost feels overwhelming.
They never actually intended to diet at all.
Diets are impossible for everyone to follow.
Future rewards feel larger than they really are.
questions.why: 當明天變成今天,眼前的成本又變得難以承受,故計畫難以執行。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention commitment devices at the end?
options: To prove that present bias cannot be overcome.
To explain how interest is calculated.
To argue that people should never make plans.
To suggest a practical way to counteract present bias.
questions.why: 結尾提到承諾機制,是為了提供對抗現時偏誤的實用方法。

talk.topic: 供需 · 供給衝擊 (supply shocks)
talk.text: Okay, let's talk about what happens when supply is suddenly disrupted, an event economists call a supply shock. A supply shock is an unexpected change that shifts the supply curve, and negative shocks are the ones that make headlines. Picture a severe frost that wipes out a large share of a country's coffee harvest. Overnight, far less coffee is available. What happens next follows straight from supply and demand. With supply sharply reduced but people wanting coffee just as much as before, the price climbs. The market rations the now-scarcer beans by raising the price until quantity demanded falls to match the smaller supply. Supply shocks aren't limited to weather. A war can cut off oil, a factory fire can halt a key component, a new regulation can raise production costs across an industry. The oil shocks of the 1970s are the textbook case; when producers slashed output, prices soared and rippled through the entire economy. Here's a subtle point worth noting. How much the price jumps depends partly on elasticity. If demand is inelastic, as it often is for essentials like fuel, buyers can't easily cut back, so the price spikes especially hard. That's why shocks to necessities tend to hurt the most.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How a sudden disruption to supply affects prices.
Why coffee is grown in cold climates.
How governments should regulate oil companies.
Why demand for fuel is always increasing.
questions.why: 全文主旨是供給衝擊,即供給突然中斷如何影響價格。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens to the price of coffee after a frost destroys much of the harvest?
options: It falls, because farmers panic and sell.
It stays the same, because demand also drops.
It becomes fixed by the government.
It climbs, because supply is reduced but demand is unchanged.
questions.why: 霜害使供給大減而需求不變,咖啡價格因此上升。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a supply shock to a good with inelastic demand?
options: The price will barely change at all.
Buyers will easily switch to substitutes.
The price is likely to spike especially hard.
The shock will have no effect on the economy.
questions.why: 需求缺乏彈性時,買家難以減少購買,價格上漲幅度會特別大。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the oil shocks of the 1970s?
options: To argue that oil is no longer important.
To give a historical example of a major supply shock.
To explain how coffee is different from oil.
To prove that regulations always lower prices.
questions.why: 提到 1970 年代石油危機,是為了舉一個重大供給衝擊的歷史實例。

talk.topic: 行銷 · 口碑與病毒行銷 (word of mouth)
talk.text: Let's turn to what many marketers consider the most persuasive force of all: word of mouth. This is simply people telling other people about a product, a friend recommending a restaurant, a colleague praising an app. Why is it so powerful? Because we trust the people we know far more than we trust advertising. A company always has an obvious motive to praise itself, but your friend has nothing to gain by steering you wrong. That credibility is something no paid ad can easily buy. In the digital age, word of mouth can scale explosively, which is what marketers mean by going viral. A single delighted customer posts a video, others share it, and within days millions have seen it, at essentially no cost to the company. But, and this is important, virality can't simply be manufactured on demand. You can't force people to share. What you can do is build something genuinely remarkable, something worth talking about, and then make sharing easy. There's a darker side, too. Word of mouth cuts both ways. An unhappy customer with a large following can damage a brand just as fast as a happy one can build it. In a connected world, the conversation about you is largely out of your hands.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: How to produce a professional advertisement.
Why restaurants are hard to run.
The power of word of mouth in marketing.
How companies calculate advertising budgets.
questions.why: 全文主旨是口碑在行銷中的力量。

questions.q: According to the professor, why do people trust word of mouth more than advertising?
options: Because advertisements are always false.
Because a friend has nothing to gain by misleading them.
Because friends are experts on every product.
Because word of mouth costs more money.
questions.why: 朋友推薦沒有利害動機,因此人們比起廣告更信任口碑。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a company that wants its product to go viral?
options: It can simply force customers to share it.
It should avoid making the product remarkable.
It can guarantee millions of views by paying more.
It should build something genuinely worth talking about.
questions.why: 由於無法強迫分享,公司應打造真正值得討論的產品才可能爆紅。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention an unhappy customer with a large following?
options: To point out that word of mouth can also harm a brand.
To explain how to gain more followers online.
To prove that customers are usually satisfied.
To describe how videos are produced.
questions.why: 提到有大量追隨者的不滿顧客,是為了指出口碑也可能傷害品牌。

talk.topic: 創業 · 先行者優勢 (first-mover advantage)
talk.text: Let's finish our unit on entrepreneurship by examining a widely debated idea: first-mover advantage. The claim is that the first company to enter a new market gains lasting benefits that later rivals struggle to overcome. What might those benefits be? A first mover can lock up scarce resources, the best locations or key suppliers. It can build brand recognition, becoming the name customers automatically associate with the product. And it can lock in customers through switching costs, making it a hassle to change to a competitor later. In some cases it also rides the network effects we discussed earlier. But here's the twist that surprises many students. Being first is often not an advantage at all. Pioneers bear enormous costs. They spend heavily to educate customers about an entirely new product, and then later entrants, so-called fast followers, swoop in and benefit from that groundwork without paying for it. Pioneers also make the early mistakes, while followers learn from them and refine the idea. History is full of first movers that were overtaken by a smarter second or third entrant. So the honest conclusion is that being first can help, but only if the company can build durable advantages before the imitators arrive. Speed alone guarantees nothing.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Why companies should never enter new markets.
How to educate customers about a product.
Why network effects always fail.
Whether being first to a market is truly an advantage.
questions.why: 全文主旨是探討率先進入市場是否真的具有優勢。

questions.q: According to the professor, what is one benefit a first mover may gain?
options: Locking up scarce resources such as the best locations.
Avoiding the cost of educating customers.
Learning from the mistakes of earlier entrants.
Paying nothing to build brand recognition.
questions.why: 先行者的一項好處是搶佔稀缺資源,例如最佳地點或關鍵供應商。

questions.q: What can be inferred about 'fast followers'?
options: They must educate customers from scratch themselves.
They can benefit from the pioneer's groundwork without paying for it.
They always fail to catch the first mover.
They enter the market before the pioneer.
questions.why: 快速追隨者能利用先行者鋪好的基礎,卻不必承擔那些成本。

questions.q: How does the professor develop the talk about first-mover advantage?
options: By claiming first movers always win in every case.
By listing the founding dates of famous companies.
By presenting the supposed benefits, then challenging them.
By describing how to register a new company.
questions.why: 教授先列出先行者的可能好處,再加以質疑,屬先立後破的結構。

talk.text: Today let's look at how Impressionism broke with the studio traditions of the nineteenth century. Before the 1870s, most French painters worked indoors, carefully finishing their canvases with smooth, invisible brushstrokes and dark, controlled lighting. The Impressionists did something radical: they carried their easels outdoors and painted directly from nature, a practice called painting 'en plein air.' This was made possible by a small technological change, paint sold in portable metal tubes, which meant artists no longer had to grind their own pigments in a workshop. Working outside, painters like Monet became obsessed with capturing fleeting effects of light and weather. Because sunlight shifts quickly, they had to work fast, using short, broken strokes of pure color rather than blended tones. Critics at first mocked these paintings as unfinished sketches. But notice what the technique achieved: by placing dabs of contrasting color side by side, the artists let the viewer's eye blend them, producing a shimmering sense of movement and atmosphere. What looked careless was actually a careful study of how we really perceive color and light in the world.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: How the Impressionists departed from studio traditions to paint light outdoors
The invention of the portable metal paint tube
Why nineteenth-century critics disliked all modern art
The differences between French and Italian painting
questions.why: 全篇圍繞印象派如何走出畫室、在戶外捕捉光線而突破傳統，(0) 最能概括主旨。

questions.q: According to the professor, what technological change helped make plein air painting possible?
options: The invention of the camera
Paint sold in portable metal tubes
New kinds of lightweight easels
Brighter artificial studio lighting
questions.why: 講稿明確提到金屬顏料管讓畫家不必在工作室自行研磨顏料，故選 (1)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why the Impressionists used short, broken brushstrokes?
options: They wanted their paintings to look unfinished on purpose
They were unable to afford fine brushes
They needed to work quickly before the light changed
They were copying older Italian fresco methods
questions.why: 講稿指出陽光變化快、畫家必須迅速作畫，可推知短促筆觸是為了趕在光線改變前完成，選 (2)。

questions.q: How does the professor make the point that the technique was not careless?
options: By comparing Monet to earlier studio painters
By quoting the critics who admired the work
By describing the metal paint tube in detail
By explaining how side-by-side colors blend in the viewer's eye
questions.why: 教授藉由說明並置的對比色會在觀者眼中融合、產生閃動感，來論證這技法其實經過精心設計，選 (3)。

talk.text: In today's session we're examining a classic worry in sociology: does city life weaken human community? In the late nineteenth century, as people poured from villages into rapidly growing cities, scholars feared that the close, personal bonds of rural life would simply dissolve. The sociologist Ferdinand Tonnies gave us two useful terms. He called the tight-knit, face-to-face relationships of a village 'Gemeinschaft,' or community, and the impersonal, contract-based relationships of the city 'Gesellschaft,' or society. In the village, he argued, people knew one another as whole persons and were bound by tradition and kinship. In the city, they interacted as strangers pursuing separate interests. Now, this is a powerful contrast, but modern research complicates it. Later studies of city neighborhoods found that urban dwellers were not, in fact, isolated. They formed strong networks, just different ones. Instead of relying on lifelong neighbors, city people built communities around workplaces, clubs, and shared interests, often scattered across the map. So the lesson is not that the city destroys community, but that it changes the shape community takes. Connection persists; it simply follows new channels.

questions.q: What is the main point of the lecture?
options: Village life is morally superior to city life
Cities do not destroy community but change its form
Ferdinand Tonnies single-handedly invented sociology
Most city dwellers today feel lonely and isolated
questions.why: 講稿最終強調城市並未摧毀社群，而是改變其形態，(1) 為主旨。

questions.q: According to Tonnies, what characterizes 'Gesellschaft'?
options: Relationships based only on kinship and tradition
A complete absence of any social ties
Impersonal, contract-based relationships
Face-to-face bonds among lifelong neighbors
questions.why: 講稿說 Gesellschaft 指城市中非個人、以契約為基礎的關係，選 (2)。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the modern research the professor mentions?
options: Tonnies's terms are no longer used by any sociologist
City neighborhoods have no social organization at all
Rural communities have completely disappeared
The early fear that cities isolate people was overstated
questions.why: 現代研究發現市民並不孤立，可推知早期「城市使人孤立」的憂慮被誇大，選 (3)。

questions.q: How does the professor develop the lecture?
options: By presenting an early theory and then showing how later research complicates it
By comparing two rival cities in close detail
By listing statistics about population growth
By describing his own experience of moving to a city
questions.why: 講者先提出 Tonnies 的早期理論，再以後續研究加以修正複雜化，屬此組織方式，選 (0)。

talk.text: Let's talk about improvisation, the beating heart of jazz. To an outsider, a jazz solo can sound like a musician simply making up notes at random. But that impression is misleading. Improvisation in jazz is more like spontaneous conversation in a language you've spent years learning. Before a soloist ever steps forward, they've internalized scales, chord progressions, and hundreds of short melodic phrases, or 'licks.' When they improvise, they draw on this vocabulary in real time, responding to the harmony of the tune and to what the other players are doing. Consider the twelve-bar blues, one of the most common frameworks. The chords follow a fixed, predictable pattern, and every musician on the bandstand knows it. That shared structure is precisely what makes freedom possible: because everyone agrees on the underlying road map, a soloist can wander freely, confident the group will stay together. So the paradox of jazz is that its famous freedom rests on deep discipline and shared rules. The most daring solo is built on a foundation the whole band silently understands.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: The twelve-bar blues is the only structure used in jazz
Jazz solos are played entirely at random
Jazz improvisation depends on discipline and shared structure
Learning jazz is impossible without formal schooling
questions.why: 全篇論證即興看似自由，實則建立在紀律與共同結構之上，選 (2)。

questions.q: What does the professor say a 'lick' is?
options: A type of jazz chord
A famous jazz musician
A particular style of drumming
A short melodic phrase
questions.why: 講稿把 lick 定義為短小的旋律樂句，選 (3)。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the twelve-bar blues?
options: To show how a shared framework allows soloists freedom
To argue that blues is harder to play than other jazz
To prove that jazz has no fixed rules at all
To describe the origins of jazz in one particular city
questions.why: 教授以十二小節藍調說明共同的和弦架構如何讓獨奏者得以自由發揮，選 (0)。

questions.q: How does the professor make his point about improvisation?
options: By playing several recordings for the class
By comparing improvisation to a conversation in a learned language
By listing the names of many famous soloists
By tracing the history of the recording industry
questions.why: 講者用「以熟習語言進行的自發對話」來類比即興，屬比喻說明，選 (1)。

talk.text: Every society we know of ranks its members in some way, and sociologists call this ranking 'social stratification.' Today I want to distinguish two basic systems for doing it. The first is a closed system, best illustrated by a caste system, in which your position is fixed at birth and cannot be changed by anything you do. You're born into a rank, you marry within it, and you die in it. The second is an open system, like the class structure of modern industrial societies. Here, position is officially based on achievement, your education, your occupation, your income, and movement between levels, what we call 'social mobility,' is at least possible.
