Now, I want to add a note of caution. Just because a class system permits mobility does not mean everyone has an equal chance to move. Children born into wealthy families inherit not only money but also connections, and confidence, and educational advantages that make upward movement far easier for them. So while open systems are more fluid than closed ones, they are not the perfectly level playing fields they sometimes claim to be.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: Why caste systems are morally unfair
The economic history of industrial societies
Practical steps for achieving upward mobility
Two basic systems of social stratification and their differences
questions.why: 講稿主要區分封閉（種姓）與開放（階級）兩種社會分層系統及其差異，選 (3)。

questions.q: According to the professor, in a caste system a person's position is:
options: Fixed at birth and unchangeable
Based mainly on education
Decided by income and occupation
Freely chosen in adulthood
questions.why: 講稿說種姓制度中地位出生即定、無法改變，選 (0)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about mobility in open class systems?
options: It is guaranteed for everyone who works hard
It is possible but not equally available to all
It happens only through marriage
It is actually rarer than in caste systems
questions.why: 講者指出富裕家庭的子女擁有更多優勢，可推知向上流動雖可能卻非人人機會均等，選 (1)。

questions.q: How is the lecture organized?
options: As a story about one family's rise to wealth
As a list of national income statistics
By contrasting two systems and then qualifying the second
By tracing stratification from ancient to modern times
questions.why: 講者先對比兩種系統，再對開放系統加以保留說明，選 (2)。

talk.text: Where did the novel come from? We tend to treat it as timeless, but the novel as we know it is a fairly recent invention, taking its modern shape in England in the early eighteenth century. Several social changes came together to make it possible. First, literacy was spreading, especially among the growing middle class, and importantly among women, who became a major reading audience. Second, printing had become cheaper, so books could be produced and sold to ordinary people rather than only to wealthy patrons. Now, what made the new novels distinctive? Unlike the old romances, full of kings, knights, and magic, novels told stories about ordinary individuals in recognizable, everyday settings, a merchant, a servant girl, a shipwrecked sailor. They used realistic detail: specific places, dates, and prices, as if reporting real events. Writers like Daniel Defoe even presented fiction as though it were a true memoir. This 'formal realism' invited readers to identify closely with characters much like themselves. So the novel didn't just appear; it answered the tastes and experiences of a new reading public that wanted to see its own life reflected on the page.

questions.q: What is the main topic of the talk?
options: The social conditions that gave rise to the modern novel
The complete life and works of Daniel Defoe
Why romances remained more popular than novels
How the printing press was first invented
questions.why: 全篇說明促成現代小說興起的社會條件，選 (0)。

questions.q: According to the professor, who became a major new audience for novels?
options: Kings and aristocratic patrons
Middle-class readers, including many women
University professors and scholars
Traveling sailors and merchants
questions.why: 講稿指出識字率上升、特別是中產階級與女性成為主要讀者，選 (1)。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that Defoe presented fiction as a true memoir?
options: To show that Defoe was dishonest with his readers
To prove that early novels were actually nonfiction
To illustrate the novel's use of realistic detail
To explain why novels were so expensive
questions.why: 教授藉此例說明小說運用寫實細節、彷彿真實紀錄的「形式寫實」，選 (2)。

questions.q: How does the professor contrast novels with older romances?
options: By comparing their selling prices
By naming the authors of each
By discussing which sold more copies
By pointing out that novels featured ordinary people in everyday settings
questions.why: 講者以「小說寫平凡人物與日常場景」對比舊式充滿國王騎士魔法的傳奇，選 (3)。

talk.text: When we ask what schools are for, the obvious answer is that they teach knowledge and skills, reading, mathematics, history. Sociologists agree that's true, but they point out that schools also teach a great deal that never appears on any syllabus. They call this the 'hidden curriculum.' Think about what a typical school day trains you to do: arrive on time, sit quietly, wait your turn, follow instructions from an authority figure, and work steadily even at tasks you didn't choose. None of these are written down as lessons, yet they're taught relentlessly, through the very structure of the school day. Why does this matter? One influential view argues that the hidden curriculum quietly prepares young people for the discipline of the workplace, especially routine jobs that demand punctuality and obedience. Critics of this view respond that it makes schools sound like factories and ignores students' capacity to question and resist. Whichever side you take, the key insight is this: to understand what a school really does, you have to look beyond the official subjects to the habits and values it transmits without ever announcing them.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: The best methods for teaching mathematics
The hidden curriculum and the unspoken lessons schools teach
Why students should always question their teachers
The historical origins of the modern school day
questions.why: 全篇圍繞「隱藏課程」——學校未列於課綱卻教導的習慣與價值，選 (1)。

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of the hidden curriculum?
options: A history lesson about ancient Rome
A mathematics exam at the end of term
Learning to arrive on time and follow instructions
A textbook chapter on grammar rules
questions.why: 隱藏課程指準時、服從指示等未明列的訓練，選 (2)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the 'influential view' the professor describes?
options: It believes schools should teach only job skills
It argues that schools have no real effect on students
It praises students for resisting authority
It sees a link between school routines and workplace discipline
questions.why: 該觀點認為隱藏課程默默為職場紀律做準備，可推知它看到校園常規與職場紀律的關聯，選 (3)。

questions.q: How does the professor present the topic?
options: By stating an obvious answer, then revealing a deeper one, and noting a debate
By narrating the history of one particular school
By listing school subjects in order of importance
By comparing schools in two different countries
questions.why: 講者先給顯而易見的答案，再揭示更深層的隱藏課程，並指出正反辯論，選 (0)。

talk.text: Let's turn to one of the great technical achievements of the Renaissance: the fresco. A fresco is a wall painting, but the word refers specifically to a method, not just a location. In 'true fresco,' or buon fresco, the artist applies pigments mixed with water onto a layer of fresh, wet plaster. As the plaster dries, a chemical reaction binds the color permanently into the wall itself. The painting doesn't sit on the surface; it becomes part of the surface. This gives fresco its remarkable durability, many have survived for five hundred years. But the method imposes a harsh discipline. The plaster stays workable only while it's damp, so the artist must finish a section within a single day, before it sets. Each day's patch is called a 'giornata,' Italian for 'a day's work.' There is no going back to make changes once the plaster dries; a mistake means chiseling out the section and starting over. So the fresco painter had to plan with extraordinary care, dividing an enormous wall into daily portions and knowing exactly what to paint before the clock, and the drying plaster, ran out.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The subjects that Renaissance artists most liked to paint
Why frescoes are now displayed in museums
The fresco method and the discipline it demands
The chemistry of modern household wall paint
questions.why: 全篇說明濕壁畫的技法及其要求的嚴格紀律，選 (2)。

questions.q: In true fresco, the pigment is applied to:
options: A dry wooden panel
A sheet of stretched canvas
A pre-painted white surface
Fresh, wet plaster
questions.why: 講稿指出濕壁畫是把顏料塗在新鮮潮濕的灰泥上，選 (3)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why frescoes last so long?
options: The color becomes chemically bonded into the wall
They are always kept in sealed, dark rooms
They are painted with unusually expensive pigments
They are regularly repainted every century
questions.why: 講稿說灰泥乾燥時的化學反應把顏色永久結合進牆體，可推知這是其耐久的原因，選 (0)。

questions.q: Why does the professor introduce the term 'giornata'?
options: To name a famous fresco painter
To emphasize that a section must be finished in one day
To describe a special type of plaster
To identify the room where frescoes are painted
questions.why: 教授引入 giornata（一日之工）以強調每塊區域必須在灰泥乾前一天內完成，選 (1)。

talk.text: Today we look at collective behavior, the ways people act when they gather in crowds. An early theorist, Gustave Le Bon, offered a dramatic picture. In a crowd, he claimed, individuals lose their sense of personal responsibility and are swept up in a kind of shared emotion, almost as if infected by a contagion. The rational person, he suggested, dissolves into an irrational mob. For a long time this 'contagion' theory dominated. But later sociologists grew skeptical. When they actually studied crowds, at protests, concerts, sporting events, they found far more order than Le Bon's theory predicted. People in crowds don't lose their minds; they continue to make choices, follow social norms, and often act with restraint. This led to what's called the 'emergent norm' theory, which argues that crowds develop their own rules on the spot, worked out through the interaction of the people present. So the modern view reverses the old one. A crowd is not a mindless organism overriding its members. It's a situation in which people, still thinking, negotiate new norms to fit unfamiliar circumstances.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Crowds are always dangerous and irrational
Gustave Le Bon was the very first sociologist
People should avoid all large gatherings
Modern theory sees crowd members as still thinking, not mindless
questions.why: 全篇最終指出現代理論翻轉舊說，認為群眾成員仍在思考、協商規範，選 (3)。

questions.q: According to Le Bon, what happens to individuals in a crowd?
options: They lose their sense of personal responsibility
They become more rational than usual
They immediately leave the group
They write down formal rules for the crowd
questions.why: 勒龐主張個人在群眾中失去個人責任感，選 (0)。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the studies of real crowds?
options: Crowds have no effect on individual behavior
Crowds show more order than Le Bon predicted
Le Bon's theory was completely confirmed
People in crowds never follow any norms
questions.why: 實地研究發現群眾比勒龐所預測更有秩序，選 (1)。

questions.q: How does the professor organize the lecture?
options: By describing a single famous protest in detail
By listing types of crowds in order of size
By presenting an old theory and the newer theory that challenges it
By comparing crowds in two different countries
questions.why: 講者先介紹勒龐的舊理論，再提出挑戰它的「突現規範」新理論，選 (2)。

talk.text: If you listen to a symphony or a string quartet from the Classical period, think Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven, the first movement is very often built on a plan called sonata form. It's worth understanding, because once you hear its shape, a huge amount of music suddenly makes sense. Sonata form has three main sections. The first is the exposition, where the composer introduces two contrasting themes, usually in different keys, often a bold, assertive first theme and a gentler, more lyrical second one. The second section is the development, and this is where the drama happens: the composer takes those themes apart, combines fragments, shifts restlessly through distant keys, and builds tension. Finally comes the recapitulation, where both themes return, but now, crucially, the second theme appears in the home key, resolving the tension the development created. You can hear the whole movement as a journey: themes are stated, thrown into conflict, and then brought home in harmony. That sense of departure and return is why sonata form felt so satisfying to Classical audiences, and why composers relied on it for generations.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The three-part structure of sonata form
The complete biography of Mozart
The difference between symphonies and quartets
How string instruments are tuned
questions.why: 全篇解說奏鳴曲式的三段結構，選 (0)。

questions.q: What happens during the development section?
options: Two brand-new themes are introduced for the first time
The themes are broken apart and moved through distant keys
The movement comes to a quiet and final end
The second theme is stated in the home key
questions.why: 講稿說發展部把主題拆解、穿梭遠系調並累積張力，選 (1)。

questions.q: Why is it significant that the second theme returns in the home key?
options: It shows the composer forgot the original key
It signals the beginning of the development
It resolves the tension built up earlier
It introduces a completely new melody
questions.why: 第二主題回到主調化解了發展部製造的張力，故其意義在於解決張力，選 (2)。

questions.q: How does the professor help students grasp sonata form?
options: By listing every symphony Haydn ever wrote
By comparing it to a famous painting
By analyzing the price of concert tickets
By describing the movement as a journey of departure and return
questions.why: 講者把整個樂章描述為「出發—衝突—回歸」的旅程，幫助理解，選 (3)。

talk.text: Here's a question that sounds simple: when people find a new job, who actually helps them do it? You might guess their closest friends and family, the people they trust most. But a famous study by the sociologist Mark Granovetter found something surprising. When he surveyed workers about how they'd landed their jobs, most had heard about the opportunity not from close friends, but from acquaintances, people they saw only occasionally. Granovetter called this 'the strength of weak ties.' Why would weak ties be so useful? Think about it this way. Your close friends tend to know the same people you know and hear the same news you hear. Their information overlaps with yours. But an acquaintance moves in different circles, talks to different people, and therefore has access to information you'd never encounter on your own. Weak ties act as bridges between separate social worlds. The lesson reshaped how sociologists think about networks. It's not just the intensity of our relationships that matters, but their reach. Sometimes the loose, casual connections do the work that our closest bonds cannot.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the talk?
options: Close friends are the most reliable source of job offers
Weak ties can provide information our close bonds cannot
Most people strongly dislike networking
Granovetter studied only unemployed workers
questions.why: 全篇論證「弱連結的力量」——熟人能提供親密關係無法提供的資訊，選 (1)。

questions.q: According to Granovetter's study, most workers heard about their jobs from:
options: Newspaper advertisements
Their closest friends and family
Acquaintances they saw only occasionally
Government employment offices
questions.why: 研究發現多數人是從偶爾往來的熟人得知工作機會，選 (2)。

questions.q: Why do close friends often provide less new information?
options: They deliberately withhold job news
They usually live too far away
They are typically unemployed themselves
They tend to know the same people and news you already know
questions.why: 親密朋友的人脈與消息與你重疊，故能提供的新資訊較少，選 (3)。

questions.q: How does the professor introduce the topic?
options: By posing a question and then challenging the obvious answer
By defining a long list of technical terms
By telling the story of his own career
By comparing two different countries' job markets
questions.why: 講者先拋出問題，再推翻「靠好友」這個直覺答案，選 (0)。

talk.text: Today I want to introduce the haiku, a Japanese poetic form famous for its brevity. In its traditional shape, a haiku consists of just seventeen sound-units, arranged in three phrases. But counting syllables is the least interesting thing about it. What makes a true haiku is something deeper. First, it almost always contains a 'kigo,' a season word, a reference to snow, cherry blossoms, cicadas, falling leaves, that anchors the poem in a particular moment of the natural year. Second, and this is crucial, a haiku usually turns on a 'kireji,' or cutting word, which creates a pause, splitting the poem into two images placed side by side. The art lies in the space between those two images. The poet does not explain the connection; the reader must feel it. Consider the master Basho, whose most famous poem sets an ancient, silent pond against the sudden splash of a frog jumping in. Nothing is interpreted for us. The poem simply presents two things, stillness and sound, and trusts the reader to sense the resonance between them. That restraint, that refusal to over-explain, is the very soul of haiku.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The personal life of the poet Basho
How to count syllables correctly in poetry
The essential features that define a true haiku
The differences between Japanese and Chinese poetry
questions.why: 全篇說明構成真正俳句的要素（季語、切字、留白），選 (2)。

questions.q: What is a 'kigo'?
options: A cutting word that creates a pause
A strict count of seventeen syllables
The name of a famous poet
A season word that sets the poem in the natural year
questions.why: 講稿定義 kigo 為指涉季節、把詩定位於自然年某刻的季語，選 (3)。

questions.q: Why does the professor discuss Basho's frog poem?
options: To illustrate how a haiku places two images side by side without explaining
To show that Basho broke the rules of haiku
To prove haiku must always mention animals
To demonstrate correct syllable counting
questions.why: 教授以芭蕉的青蛙詩示範俳句如何並置兩個意象而不加解釋，選 (0)。

questions.q: How does the professor emphasize what truly matters in haiku?
options: By reciting the poem in the original Japanese
By saying syllable-counting is the least interesting feature
By comparing haiku to Western sonnets
By listing every season word in order
questions.why: 講者以「數音節是最不重要的一點」來凸顯俳句真正的精髓在別處，選 (1)。

talk.text: What makes an act 'deviant'? The commonsense answer is that some behaviors are simply wrong in themselves. But sociologists offer a more unsettling idea. According to labeling theory, no act is inherently deviant; an act becomes deviant only when a society successfully labels it that way. The same behavior can be celebrated in one place and punished in another, or accepted in one era and condemned in the next. What matters is the social reaction. Now, this theory has a second, sharper claim. The label itself can shape a person's future. Imagine a teenager caught in a minor act of rule-breaking who is then publicly labeled a 'delinquent.' Teachers watch him more closely, respectable friends drift away, and he's pushed toward others who share the label. Over time, he may come to see himself as the label says he is, and act accordingly. Sociologists call this a 'self-fulfilling prophecy.' So labeling theory shifts our attention. Instead of asking only why individuals break rules, it asks who has the power to apply labels, and what those labels do to the people who receive them.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: The reasons why teenagers break rules
The history of criminal punishment
Practical ways to reduce crime in cities
Labeling theory and how social reactions define deviance
questions.why: 全篇圍繞標籤理論及社會反應如何界定偏差行為，選 (3)。

questions.q: According to labeling theory, an act becomes deviant when:
options: society successfully labels it as deviant
it causes physical harm to someone
it breaks a clearly written law
it is committed by a teenager
questions.why: 標籤理論主張行為要在社會成功貼上標籤後才成為偏差，選 (0)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the labeled teenager in the example?
options: He will certainly stop breaking rules
The label may push him toward further deviance
He was never actually labeled at all
His teachers will simply ignore his behavior
questions.why: 例中標籤導致他被孤立、向同類靠攏，可推知標籤可能推他走向更多偏差，選 (1)。

questions.q: How does the professor develop the idea of labeling theory?
options: By quoting detailed crime statistics
By comparing the laws of two countries
By stating the theory and then illustrating it with an example
By describing his own experience as a teacher
questions.why: 講者先陳述理論，再以青少年被貼標籤的例子加以說明，選 (2)。

talk.text: After the Second World War, the center of the art world shifted from Paris to New York, and the movement that announced this shift was Abstract Expressionism. I want to focus on one of its most radical figures, Jackson Pollock. Pollock did something that overturned centuries of practice. Instead of standing at an upright easel and touching brush to canvas, he laid a huge, unstretched canvas flat on the floor and moved around all four sides of it, dripping and flinging liquid paint from sticks and hardened brushes. Critics nicknamed him 'Jack the Dripper.' Now, it's tempting to dismiss this as chaos, paint thrown at random. But look closely and you'll see rhythm and control. Pollock decided where to stand, how fast to move, how much paint to load, when to let a line thicken or trail off. The finished works record the artist's physical movements, like the trace of a dance. This is why the approach is sometimes called 'action painting.' The subject of the painting, in a sense, is the very act of making it, the energy and gesture of the body captured on the canvas.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: Jackson Pollock's radical painting method and its meaning
Why Paris declined as an art center
The political causes of the Second World War
How artists' canvases are manufactured
questions.why: 全篇聚焦波洛克的激進畫法及其意義，選 (0)。

questions.q: How did Pollock apply paint to his canvases?
options: With a fine brush at an upright easel
By dripping and flinging paint onto canvas laid on the floor
By pressing carved wooden blocks onto them
By spraying paint through a machine
questions.why: 講稿說波洛克把畫布平鋪地上、以滴灑甩動的方式上色，選 (1)。

questions.q: What does the professor suggest about the apparent chaos of Pollock's work?
options: It proves the paintings were made purely by accident
It shows Pollock lacked any artistic training
It actually reflects rhythm and deliberate control
It means the works have no lasting value
questions.why: 講者指出細看可見節奏與控制，可推知看似混亂實則有意為之，選 (2)。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that the paintings record Pollock's movements?
options: To criticize Pollock for being careless
To explain how paint is chemically made
To argue that Paris was still the art capital
To explain why the approach is called 'action painting'
questions.why: 講者說畫作記錄了畫家的身體動作，以此解釋為何稱為「行動繪畫」，選 (3)。

talk.topic: The family as a social institution
talk.text: When we think of 'family,' many of us picture a particular arrangement: two parents and their children living together in one household. Sociologists call this the 'nuclear family.' But I want to challenge the assumption that this is the natural or universal form of family. Across human societies and throughout history, a far more common arrangement has been the 'extended family,' a household or close network including grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins, often living together or nearby and sharing daily labor and childcare. The nuclear family, small and relatively independent, became widespread in the West largely alongside industrialization. As factories drew workers into cities and demanded that people move wherever jobs were, a large, rooted extended household became harder to maintain. A smaller, more mobile family unit fit the new economy better. So the point I want you to take away is this: the form a family takes is not fixed by nature. It responds to economic and social conditions. What one society treats as the obvious, normal family may be, in the long view of history, quite unusual.

questions.q: What is the main point of the lecture?
options: The nuclear family is the healthiest form of family
Family forms are shaped by social and economic conditions, not fixed by nature
Extended families no longer exist anywhere today
Industrialization was fundamentally harmful to families
questions.why: 全篇主旨在於家庭形態受社會經濟條件塑造、並非自然固定，選 (1)。

questions.q: According to the professor, an 'extended family' includes:
options: only two parents and their children
a single parent living entirely alone
relatives such as grandparents, aunts, and cousins
unrelated workers sharing a factory dormitory
questions.why: 講稿把擴展家庭定義為包含祖父母、姑姨、堂表親等親屬，選 (2)。

questions.q: Why did the nuclear family spread in the West?
options: Because governments passed laws requiring it
Because extended families were made illegal
Because people simply preferred smaller, more comfortable homes
Because industrial work required a smaller, more mobile unit
questions.why: 講稿說工業化要求勞工遷移，較小而靈活的家庭更適應新經濟，選 (3)。

questions.q: How does the professor build the argument?
options: By challenging a common assumption and giving a historical explanation
By listing the members of his own family
By comparing families in two neighboring towns
By presenting survey statistics on household size
questions.why: 講者先挑戰「核心家庭是自然普世」的假設，再給出歷史解釋，選 (0)。

talk.text: To understand twentieth-century popular music, you have to start with the blues. The blues emerged among African American communities in the American South in the decades after the Civil War, growing out of work songs, spirituals, and field hollers. Musically, it has some distinctive features. One is the 'blue note,' a note deliberately bent or sung slightly lower than the standard scale, giving the music its aching, expressive quality. Another is the 'call and response' pattern, where a vocal line is answered by an instrument, echoing older communal singing traditions. Now, here's why the blues matters far beyond its own recordings. Its structures and feeling became the foundation for an astonishing range of later music. Jazz drew on its harmonies. Rhythm and blues grew directly out of it. And in the 1950s, when the blues was combined with country music and amplified with electric guitars, the result was rock and roll. So a music born in hardship, in a specific community and time, ended up shaping the sound of the entire century. When you hear a bent guitar note in a rock song today, you're hearing the blues.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The political causes of the American Civil War
How electric guitars are built and wired
The features of the blues and its influence on later music
The biography of a single famous blues singer
questions.why: 全篇說明藍調的特徵及其對後世音樂的影響，選 (2)。

questions.q: What is a 'blue note'?
options: A note that can be played only on the piano
A very loud, heavily amplified note
The final note of every blues song
A note bent or sung slightly lower than the standard scale
questions.why: 講稿定義藍調音為刻意壓低、彎曲的音，選 (3)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about rock and roll?
options: It grew partly out of the blues
It existed long before the blues
It has no connection to country music
It replaced the blues entirely by 1900
questions.why: 講稿說 1950 年代藍調與鄉村樂結合並電氣化而生搖滾樂，可推知搖滾部分源自藍調，選 (0)。

questions.q: How does the professor end the lecture?
options: By listing the exact dates of famous recordings
By noting that a bent guitar note in rock still echoes the blues
By comparing the blues to classical symphonies
By explaining step by step how to play a blue note
questions.why: 講者以「今日搖滾中彎曲的吉他音仍是藍調的回響」作結，選 (1)。

talk.text: Let's consider what globalization does to local cultures. One widely shared worry is captured by a grim phrase: cultural homogenization. The fear is that as the same films, brands, and fast food spread to every corner of the planet, distinct local cultures will be flattened into one bland, uniform global culture, that the whole world will start to look and taste the same. There's clearly something to this. But sociologists who study the process closely have noticed that it's rarely so simple. When a global product arrives in a local setting, people don't just passively swallow it. They adapt it, reinterpret it, and blend it with local traditions. A global fast-food chain may add dishes to suit regional tastes; a foreign style of music gets fused with local rhythms and languages. Scholars call this blending 'glocalization,' a combination of 'global' and 'local.' The result is not simple sameness, but new hybrid forms. So the relationship between the global and the local is a two-way street. Global culture reshapes local life, yes, but local cultures also absorb and transform what arrives, producing something genuinely new.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Global fast food is generally unhealthy
Local cultures will soon disappear completely
Globalization should be stopped by governments
Local cultures adapt global products rather than simply being erased
questions.why: 全篇主旨在於在地文化會吸收轉化全球產品而非單純被抹去，選 (3)。

questions.q: What does the term 'glocalization' describe?
options: The blending of global products with local traditions
The complete disappearance of local culture
The spread of one single language worldwide
A government ban on foreign products
questions.why: 講稿說 glocalization 指全球與在地的融合，選 (0)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the fear of cultural homogenization?
options: It has been completely proven correct
It captures part of the truth but oversimplifies it
It was invented by fast-food companies
It applies only to popular music
questions.why: 講者說這憂慮「有其道理」但過程「鮮少如此簡單」，可推知它只捕捉部分真相、過度簡化，選 (1)。

questions.q: How does the professor structure the lecture?
options: By narrating the history of one company
By listing many countries in alphabetical order
By presenting a common fear and then complicating it
By comparing two unrelated theories of economics
questions.why: 講者先提出「文化同質化」的普遍憂慮，再加以複雜化修正，選 (2)。

talk.text: Let's explore Gothic literature, a genre that emerged in the late eighteenth century and still shapes horror stories today. Gothic fiction is recognizable first by its atmosphere and setting. Think of crumbling castles, ruined abbeys, dark forests, and gloomy weather, places that are ancient, decaying, and full of shadows. These settings aren't just decoration. They create a mood of dread and mystery, and they suggest that the past is not truly dead but reaches out to haunt the present. That idea of the haunting past is central. Gothic stories are full of buried secrets, family curses, and ghosts, literal or figurative, that return to disturb the living. But here's what I find most interesting. Many scholars argue that these supernatural terrors are really about psychology. The ghosts and monsters externalize inner fears, guilt, desire, madness, things the characters cannot face directly. The crumbling house may mirror a crumbling mind. So when you read a Gothic tale, look past the surface machinery of chains and thunderstorms. Ask what hidden fear the horror is giving shape to. The genre endures precisely because it dramatizes anxieties we all recognize but would rather not name.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The characteristic features of Gothic literature and what they express
The architecture of real medieval castles
The biography of one famous horror writer
The history of how novels were printed
questions.why: 全篇說明哥德文學的特徵及其所表達的內涵，選 (0)。

questions.q: Which of the following is a typical Gothic setting mentioned by the professor?
options: A bright, modern city center
A crumbling castle or ruined abbey
A crowded daytime marketplace
A sunny seaside resort
questions.why: 講稿舉出破敗的城堡、荒廢的修道院等作為典型場景，選 (1)。

questions.q: What do many scholars believe the supernatural elements really represent?
options: Actual historical events
Practical advice for building safer houses
The characters' inner fears and psychology
Religious instructions for readers
questions.why: 講者說學者認為超自然恐怖其實外化角色內在的恐懼與心理，選 (2)。

questions.q: How does the professor conclude the lecture?
options: By listing every Gothic novel ever written
By describing the stormy weather in great detail
By comparing Gothic fiction to lyric poetry
By urging readers to ask what hidden fear the horror gives shape to
questions.why: 講者以「閱讀時要追問恐怖為哪種隱藏恐懼賦形」作結，選 (3)。

talk.text: Why do social movements succeed or fail? For a long time, scholars assumed the answer lay in grievances, that movements arise simply because people are suffering or angry. But this explanation runs into a problem. Suffering and discontent are extremely common, yet organized movements are relatively rare. Grievances alone, it turns out, don't predict when people will actually mobilize. So sociologists developed a different approach called 'resource mobilization theory.' Its key insight is that discontent must be organized to become effective, and organization requires resources. What kind of resources? Money, certainly, but also time, skilled leaders, communication networks, and access to media. A movement with deeply felt grievances but no resources tends to fizzle, while one that can marshal people, funds, and organizational know-how can turn widespread frustration into sustained action. Notice how this shifts the question. We stop asking merely 'Are people angry?' and start asking 'Can they organize? Do they have the tools?' It reminds us that a movement is not just a mood; it's an achievement, something built, deliberately, out of resources that not every aggrieved group possesses.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: A list of the world's most famous protest movements
Why resources, not just grievances, determine a movement's success
How governments typically suppress protests
The emotional experience of being inside a crowd
questions.why: 全篇主旨為資源（而不只是不滿）決定運動成敗，選 (1)。

questions.q: Which of the following counts as a 'resource' in the theory described?
options: A shared feeling of anger
A general sense of injustice
Skilled leaders and communication networks
A sudden rise in unemployment
questions.why: 講稿把技術嫻熟的領袖、溝通網絡列為資源，選 (2)。

questions.q: What problem with the 'grievances' explanation does the professor point out?
options: Grievances are impossible to measure
People never feel genuine grievances
Grievances always lead directly to violence
Discontent is common, yet organized movements are rare
questions.why: 講者指出不滿普遍存在、但有組織的運動卻罕見，說明單靠不滿無法解釋動員，選 (3)。

questions.q: How does the professor develop the lecture?
options: By rejecting one explanation and introducing a better one
By narrating the story of a single activist
By comparing two neighboring countries
By listing resources in order of their cost
questions.why: 講者先否定「不滿論」，再提出更佳的「資源動員論」，選 (0)。

talk.text: Let's finish with ukiyo-e, the Japanese woodblock prints that flourished in the Edo period, roughly the seventeenth through nineteenth centuries. The name ukiyo-e means 'pictures of the floating world,' referring to the fashionable, pleasure-seeking urban culture the prints depicted: kabuki actors, popular entertainers, and famous landscapes. What I want to stress is that these were not rare, precious paintings. They were affordable prints, produced in large numbers for ordinary townspeople, a genuinely popular art. And producing them was a team effort. We often credit a single artist, like Hokusai, but a finished print required at least four specialists working together: the artist who drew the design, a carver who cut the design into wooden blocks, a printer who inked the blocks and pressed the paper, and a publisher who financed and coordinated the whole enterprise. A separate block had to be carved for each color. Now here's a striking historical footnote. When these prints reached Europe in the nineteenth century, their bold flat colors and daring compositions astonished Western artists and helped inspire the Impressionists. So a popular art made for the townspeople of Edo ended up reshaping the course of European painting.

questions.q: What is the talk mainly about?
options: The personal life story of the artist Hokusai
How paper was manufactured in old Japan
Ukiyo-e woodblock prints, how they were made, and their influence
The politics and rulers of the Edo period
questions.why: 全篇說明浮世繪的性質、製作與影響，選 (2)。

questions.q: According to the professor, producing a print required:
options: a single artist working entirely alone
expensive imported materials
only two people, an artist and a printer
a team of at least four specialists
questions.why: 講稿說一幅版畫需畫師、雕版師、刷版師與出版商至少四位專家合作，選 (3)。

questions.q: What can be inferred about who bought ukiyo-e prints?
options: They were ordinary townspeople, not just the wealthy
They were only European collectors
They were exclusively members of the ruling class
They were mostly foreign tourists
questions.why: 講稿強調版畫價格親民、大量生產供一般市民，可推知買家是平民百姓，選 (0)。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention that the prints reached Europe?
options: To explain in detail how woodblocks are carved
To show that this popular art influenced Western painting
To argue that European art was ultimately superior
To describe the changing price of the prints
questions.why: 講者提及版畫傳入歐洲，以說明這門大眾藝術影響了西方繪畫，選 (1)。

talk.topic: The Silk Road as a network
talk.text: OK, so today I want to talk about the Silk Road, and the first thing I want you to do is forget the name a little bit. Because when we say 'road,' you probably picture a single highway running from China to the Mediterranean. That's not really what it was. It was a whole web of overland routes, shifting over time, and honestly most traders never traveled the entire length. Goods moved in stages, passing from one merchant to the next, from one oasis town to the next. So a bolt of silk might change hands a dozen times before it reached Rome. Now, here's what I find fascinating. The most important cargo wasn't silk, or spices, or gold. It was ideas. Religions traveled these routes: Buddhism moved from India into China this way. Technologies moved too, papermaking being a big one. And, unfortunately, so did disease. So when we study the Silk Road, we're really studying one of the earliest examples of what we'd now call globalization, centuries before the word existed.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: The true nature of the Silk Road as a network of exchange
The methods used to produce silk in ancient China
The military conflicts that closed the Silk Road
A single merchant's journey from China to Rome
questions.why: 教授整堂課強調絲路並非單一道路，而是傳遞貨物與思想的交流網絡，故主旨為 A。

questions.q: According to the professor, how did most goods move along the Silk Road?
options: They were carried by a single merchant the entire distance
They passed in stages from one merchant to the next
They were transported mainly by sea
They were shipped directly from oasis towns to Rome
questions.why: 教授說貨物「in stages, passing from one merchant to the next」，多次易手，故選 B。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the term 'globalization'?
options: It was first used by Silk Road merchants
It applies only to modern economies
The processes it describes existed long before the term
It refers mainly to the spread of disease
questions.why: 教授說絲路是「globalization」的早期例子，且「centuries before the word existed」，可推論該現象早於詞彙出現，故 C。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention papermaking?
options: To explain how silk was manufactured
To show why the routes eventually declined
To contrast Chinese and Roman technology
To give an example of technology spreading along the routes
questions.why: 教授舉造紙術為「technologies moved too」的例子，用以說明技術沿絲路傳播，故 D。

talk.text: Alright, let's get into dating methods, and I want to start with one of my favorites: dendrochronology. That's a big word, but it just means tree-ring dating. Here's the basic idea. Every year a tree grows, it adds a new ring of wood, and the thickness of that ring depends on the weather. A wet year, a fat ring. A dry year, a thin ring. Now, the clever part: trees growing in the same region experience the same weather, so they share the same pattern of fat and thin rings. It's like a barcode that the climate stamps onto every tree. So what archaeologists do is match the ring pattern from a wooden beam in, say, an old building to a master sequence built from many overlapping samples, some from living trees, some from ancient wood. And if you do it right, you don't just get an approximate age. You can pin down the exact year the tree was cut. That kind of precision is incredibly rare in archaeology, which is why we get so excited about it.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How trees adapt to changing climates
A dating technique based on tree rings
The construction of ancient wooden buildings
How to build a master sequence of samples
questions.why: 全課介紹以年輪定年（dendrochronology）這項技術，故主旨為 B。

questions.q: According to the professor, what determines the thickness of a tree ring?
options: The age of the tree
The type of soil
The weather during that year
The species of the tree
questions.why: 教授明確說厚薄取決於天氣：濕年厚環、乾年薄環，故 C。

questions.q: What can be inferred about dendrochronology compared with other dating methods?
options: It works on any material found at a site
It is less reliable than guessing
It can only date living trees
It offers unusually precise dates
questions.why: 教授說能定出「exact year」，並稱這種精確度在考古中「incredibly rare」，可推論它比多數方法更精確，故 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor compare tree rings to a barcode?
options: To illustrate how climate creates a matchable pattern
To show that trees can be bought and sold
To emphasize how quickly trees grow
To explain why rings are hard to read
questions.why: 教授用「barcode」比喻氣候在每棵樹上蓋下可比對的圖案，故 A。

talk.text: So, one of the first things anthropologists study when they enter a new community is kinship, meaning how people are related and, more importantly, how they think they're related. And I stress 'think,' because kinship isn't just biology. Let me give you an example. In many societies, the word we'd translate as 'father' doesn't point to just one man. It might apply to your father and all of his brothers. They're all 'father' to you. And you'd owe them all the same respect, the same obligations. Now why does this matter? Because kinship organizes everything. It tells you who you can marry, who you can't, who inherits property, who you're expected to support in a dispute. In societies without written laws or formal governments, kinship is the government. It's the legal system, the welfare system, all rolled into one. So when an anthropologist maps out a kinship chart, they're not just drawing a family tree for fun. They're uncovering the hidden architecture that holds the whole society together.

questions.q: What is the main point of the lecture?
options: How to translate kinship terms accurately
Why written laws are better than kinship
How kinship organizes many aspects of a society
The biological basis of family relationships
questions.why: 教授強調親屬關係組織了社會的方方面面，是社會的「隱藏架構」，故主旨 C。

questions.q: In the professor's example, who might be called 'father'?
options: Only the biological father
Any older man in the village
The community leader
The father and all of his brothers
questions.why: 教授舉例說「father」可指父親與其所有兄弟，故 D。

questions.q: What can be inferred about societies without formal governments?
options: Kinship performs functions that laws and courts do elsewhere
They have no rules about marriage
They cannot resolve disputes
They rely mainly on written records
questions.why: 教授說在沒有成文法或政府的社會裡「kinship is the government」，可推論親屬承擔了他處由法律法院承擔的功能，故 A。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention marriage and inheritance?
options: To argue that kinship is purely biological
To illustrate the many things kinship controls
To compare two different societies
To explain how kinship charts are drawn
questions.why: 教授列舉婚配、繼承等，用以說明親屬關係支配的眾多事務，故 B。

talk.text: OK, imagine you're digging a hole at an archaeological site, and as you go down, you notice the dirt changes. There's a dark layer, then a sandy layer, then a layer full of ash. Each of these is what we call a stratum, and studying them is stratigraphy. Now the fundamental principle here is beautifully simple. It's called the law of superposition, and it says: in an undisturbed site, the layers on the bottom were deposited first, so they're older, and the layers on top came later, so they're younger. Think about your laundry basket. The shirt at the bottom is the one you threw in on Monday. The one on top is from Friday. Same idea. So even before we can put an exact date on anything, stratigraphy gives us relative dating. We know the order of events. But, and this is important, I said 'undisturbed.' Animals dig burrows. People dig pits. Floods rearrange things. So a good archaeologist has to read the layers carefully to spot where the neat sequence has been mixed up.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How to dig a hole safely at a site
The exact dating of ancient objects
How floods destroy archaeological sites
Using soil layers to determine relative age
questions.why: 全課講地層學如何靠土層判斷相對年代，故主旨 D。

questions.q: According to the law of superposition, where are the oldest layers found?
options: At the bottom
At the top
In the middle
Near animal burrows
questions.why: 疊置定律指底層最先沉積、最古老，故 A。

questions.q: Why does the professor emphasize the word 'undisturbed'?
options: Because most sites are perfectly preserved
Because disturbances can reverse or mix the layer sequence
Because animals never affect archaeological sites
Because exact dates are always available
questions.why: 教授指出動物挖洞、人挖坑、洪水都會擾動層序，故強調「未受擾動」很重要，可推論擾動會打亂順序，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention a laundry basket?
options: To explain how sites get contaminated
To show that archaeology is like housework
To provide a familiar analogy for superposition
To describe a type of stratum
questions.why: 洗衣籃比喻幫助理解「底層較舊、上層較新」的疊置原理，故 C。

talk.text: Let's talk about history before writing, because a lot of students assume that if a society didn't write things down, it had no history, no way to remember its past. That's just wrong. These societies used oral tradition, and some of them were remarkably good at it. Now, you might be thinking, 'Come on, if you pass a story down by word of mouth for two hundred years, it's going to turn into nonsense, like a game of telephone.' But here's the thing: many oral traditions had built-in safeguards. Stories were put into rhyme or song, because rhythm is much easier to memorize accurately. Specially trained individuals, sometimes a whole class of them, were responsible for reciting genealogies or epics word for word, and if they made a mistake, others would correct them. In some cultures the recitation was tied to a ceremony, so errors would be, well, almost sacrilegious. So oral tradition wasn't sloppy. It was a genuine technology for storing information, just one that used human memory instead of paper.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Oral tradition was a reliable method of preserving the past
Writing is always superior to memory
Most oral stories become nonsense over time
Ceremonies were the main form of entertainment
questions.why: 教授全篇論證口述傳統是可靠的資訊保存方式，故主旨 A。

questions.q: According to the professor, why were stories often put into rhyme or song?
options: To make them more entertaining
To hide their meaning from outsiders
Because rhythm makes accurate memorization easier
Because writing was forbidden
questions.why: 教授說押韻或歌唱是因為節奏更易精確記憶，故 C。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the 'game of telephone' comparison?
options: It accurately describes all oral traditions
The professor uses it to dismiss a common misconception
It proves oral tradition is unreliable
It was a real ceremony in some cultures
questions.why: 教授先提出「傳話遊戲」的常見假設，再以安全機制反駁它，可推論這比喻是被反駁的誤解，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention trained reciters correcting each other?
options: To show that oral tradition was only for entertainment
To explain how writing developed
To argue that memory is unreliable
To give an example of a safeguard ensuring accuracy
questions.why: 專門背誦者互相糾錯是口述傳統「built-in safeguards」的例子，用以說明其準確性，故 D。

talk.text: Now we come to probably the most famous dating method in all of archaeology: radiocarbon dating. Here's how it works, and I'll keep the chemistry light. While a plant or animal is alive, it's constantly taking in carbon from the environment, and a tiny fraction of that carbon is a radioactive form called carbon-14. So a living thing has a steady, known amount of carbon-14 in it. But the moment it dies, it stops taking in new carbon, and the carbon-14 it already has begins to decay at a fixed, predictable rate. So if I hand you a piece of ancient charcoal or bone, you measure how much carbon-14 is left, compare it to how much there should have been, and the difference tells you how long ago the organism died. Now there's a catch. Carbon-14 decays fairly quickly, on a geological scale. After about fifty thousand years, there's almost none left to measure. So radiocarbon is fantastic for the human past, the last few tens of thousands of years, but useless for dating, say, dinosaurs. For those you need entirely different methods.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: The chemistry of living plants
How radiocarbon dating determines the age of organic remains
Why dinosaurs cannot be studied
The difference between charcoal and bone
questions.why: 全課解說放射性碳定年如何測定有機遺骸年代，故主旨 B。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happens to carbon-14 when an organism dies?
options: It increases rapidly
It stays exactly the same forever
It turns into a plant
It stops being replenished and begins to decay
questions.why: 教授說生物一死就停止攝入新碳，已有的碳-14 開始衰變，故 D。

questions.q: Why is radiocarbon dating unsuitable for dating dinosaurs?
options: Too little carbon-14 remains after tens of thousands of years
Dinosaurs contained no carbon at all
The method is too expensive to use
Dinosaur bones are too large to sample
questions.why: 教授說約五萬年後幾乎沒有碳-14 可測，恐龍太古老，可推論碳-14 已衰變殆盡，故 A。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention dinosaurs?
options: To explain how carbon-14 forms
To describe a new discovery
To illustrate the method's time limit
To compare bone and charcoal
questions.why: 提恐龍是為凸顯碳定年的時間上限（約五萬年），故 C。

talk.text: So when Columbus crossed the Atlantic in 1492, we usually frame it as a story about explorers and empires. But I want you to think about it biologically, because what happened next, historians call the Columbian Exchange, was one of the biggest transformations in the history of life on this planet. For millions of years, the Americas and the rest of the world had been developing separately, with different plants, animals, and diseases. Suddenly they were connected. Think about the food on your plate. Tomatoes in Italian cooking? Those came from the Americas. Potatoes in Ireland? The Americas. Chili peppers in Thai and Indian food? All from the Americas, and all arrived after 1492. And it went the other way too: horses, wheat, and cattle went to the New World. But here's the dark side. The exchange also moved diseases, and Indigenous American populations had no immunity to things like smallpox. In some regions, ninety percent of the population died. So the Columbian Exchange reshaped diets, economies, and, tragically, entire civilizations, all because two biological worlds were suddenly stitched together.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: Columbus's personal biography
The best recipes using tomatoes
The biological consequences of connecting two worlds after 1492
How horses were first domesticated
questions.why: 全課論述哥倫布大交換帶來的生物層面巨變，故主旨 C。

questions.q: According to the professor, which of these foods originated in the Americas?
options: Tomatoes, potatoes, and chili peppers
Wheat and cattle
Horses and rice
Coffee and tea
questions.why: 教授明說番茄、馬鈴薯、辣椒皆源自美洲，故 A。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why disease was so deadly to Indigenous Americans?
options: They lived in very cold climates
They had no food to eat
They refused all medical help
They had no prior immunity to the new diseases
questions.why: 教授說原住民對天花等「had no immunity」，可推論缺乏免疫力使疾病致命，故 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor ask students to think about the food on their plate?
options: To recommend a healthy diet
To make the scale of the exchange concrete and familiar
To explain cooking techniques
To criticize Italian cuisine
questions.why: 用餐盤上的食物讓學生具體感受大交換的規模與影響，故 B。

talk.text: Alright, let's talk about Pompeii, the Roman town buried when Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD. Now, from a purely human standpoint it was a catastrophe. But from an archaeological standpoint, and I say this with some guilt, it was extraordinary. Here's why. Most ancient towns decayed slowly. Buildings collapsed, wood rotted, people carried off anything valuable, and what's left is a picked-over ruin. Pompeii was different. It was buried fast, under meters of volcanic ash, and then just, sealed. So archaeologists found loaves of bread still in the ovens. They found election slogans painted on walls, graffiti, the layout of shops and homes frozen exactly as they were on an ordinary afternoon. Now here's the really haunting part. The ash hardened around the bodies of victims, and over centuries the bodies decayed, leaving hollow cavities. When excavators poured plaster into those cavities, they got casts of people in their final moments. So Pompeii gives us something rare: not a grand monument built to impress, but a snapshot of everyday Roman life, caught by accident.

questions.q: What is the main point of the lecture?
options: Volcanoes are the greatest threat to cities
Romans enjoyed painting graffiti
How to make plaster casts
Why Pompeii offers a rare snapshot of everyday Roman life
questions.why: 教授重點在龐貝因迅速掩埋而保存了日常生活的罕見樣貌，故主旨 D。

questions.q: According to the professor, why is Pompeii different from most ancient towns?
options: It was never inhabited
It was buried quickly and sealed by ash
It was built entirely of stone
It was carefully preserved by its residents
questions.why: 教授說龐貝被火山灰迅速掩埋並封存，不同於慢慢衰敗的城鎮，故 B。

questions.q: How were the plaster casts of victims created?
options: The Romans made them before the eruption
They were carved from volcanic rock
Plaster was poured into cavities left by decayed bodies
They were painted onto the walls
questions.why: 教授說遺體腐化後留下空腔，灌入石膏便得到人形鑄模，故 C。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention bread in the ovens and election slogans?
options: To show how completely ordinary life was preserved
To prove Romans were skilled bakers
To explain how the volcano erupted
To describe Roman political systems
questions.why: 烤爐裡的麵包與競選標語都是日常生活被完整保存的例證，故 A。

talk.text: Today I want to challenge an assumption you probably carry around without noticing: the idea that giving things away makes you poorer. Among several Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest coast, there was a ceremony called the potlatch, and at a potlatch, a host would give away, or sometimes even destroy, enormous amounts of property. Blankets, food, valuable coppers. Now to an outside observer this looked like madness, or pure waste. Early officials actually banned it. But they'd completely misread what was happening. In this system, wealth wasn't something you hoarded to display status. Status came from giving. The more lavishly you gave, the higher your prestige, and the more your rivals were obligated to give even more at their own potlatch to keep up. So the goods circulated through the whole society, and generosity, not accumulation, was the measure of a great person. It's a powerful reminder that 'economy' doesn't mean the same thing everywhere. Our assumptions about what's rational are, themselves, cultural.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: In some cultures, status comes from giving rather than accumulating wealth
The potlatch was an economically irrational practice
Governments were right to ban the potlatch
Blankets were the most valuable trade goods
questions.why: 教授核心論點是某些文化中地位來自慷慨給予而非積累，故主旨 A。

questions.q: According to the professor, what happened to a host's prestige when he gave lavishly?
options: It decreased
It stayed the same
It was transferred to the government
It increased
questions.why: 教授說給得越豪奢，聲望越高，故 D。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the officials who banned the potlatch?
options: They understood the ceremony perfectly
They were themselves potlatch hosts
They misunderstood the culture's idea of wealth
They wanted to increase Indigenous prestige
questions.why: 教授說官員「completely misread」這現象，可推論他們誤解了該文化對財富的觀念，故 C。

questions.q: Why does the professor say 'economy doesn't mean the same thing everywhere'?
options: To argue that all economies are irrational
To draw a broader lesson about cultural assumptions
To explain how to become wealthy
To describe the value of coppers
questions.why: 教授藉此帶出更廣的結論：對「理性」的看法本身是文化建構的，故 B。

talk.text: Let's go back, way back, to the Ice Age, and talk about cave art. The most famous example is Lascaux in France, where the walls are covered with paintings of horses, bulls, deer, done maybe seventeen thousand years ago. And these aren't crude stick figures. They're sophisticated. The artists used the natural bulges of the rock to give the animals a three-dimensional feel, and they clearly understood movement, proportion, perspective. So the first thing this tells us is that people that long ago were, artistically, every bit as capable as we are. But here's the puzzle that keeps researchers up at night: why did they make them? These paintings are often deep inside caves, in chambers that are hard to reach, pitch dark, not where anyone lived. So they probably weren't just decoration. Some scholars think they were part of hunting rituals. Others suggest they mark sacred spaces, or record myths, or were used in ceremonies to teach the young. The honest answer is we don't fully know. And there's something wonderful about standing in front of art that powerful and still being unable to read its meaning.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How to reach deep cave chambers
The sophistication and mystery of Ice Age cave art
The extinction of Ice Age animals
Modern techniques for painting on rock
questions.why: 全課討論冰河時期洞穴藝術的精巧與其未解之謎，故主旨 B。

questions.q: According to the professor, how did the artists create a three-dimensional feel?
options: By using the natural bulges of the rock
By painting only at cave entrances
By using bright artificial light
By carving deep grooves
questions.why: 教授說藝術家利用岩石自然凸起營造立體感，故 A。

questions.q: What can be inferred from the paintings being deep inside dark caves?
options: People lived in those chambers
The art was meant for daily decoration
The artists could not paint outdoors
The paintings likely served a special, non-decorative purpose
questions.why: 教授指畫作位於難達的黑暗深處、非居住處，故推論其可能具儀式等特殊用途，故 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor say we 'don't fully know' why the art was made?
options: To criticize earlier researchers
To prove the paintings are fake
To acknowledge an unresolved question that still intrigues scholars
To suggest the topic is not worth studying
questions.why: 教授坦言動機成謎，帶出仍令學者著迷的未解問題，故 C。

talk.text: So how does an anthropologist actually study a culture? You might imagine handing out surveys, or interviewing a few people and flying home. But the signature method of our field is something much more demanding: participant observation. The idea, pioneered by Bronislaw Malinowski about a century ago, is that you go and live with the people you're studying, ideally for a year or more. You learn the language. You eat what they eat. You take part in daily life while also carefully observing and recording it. And that's the tension built right into the name, isn't it? You're a participant, involved, and an observer, standing back. Why go to all this trouble? Because people often can't tell you the rules they live by, any more than you could explain every rule of grammar you follow when you speak. Much of culture is unspoken, taken for granted. By living inside it, the anthropologist can notice patterns that even the members of the culture aren't consciously aware of. The downside, of course, is that it's slow, it's subjective, and the observer inevitably changes the very situation they're watching.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How to design a good survey
The biography of Bronislaw Malinowski
Participant observation as the core method of anthropology
Why learning languages is difficult
questions.why: 全課介紹參與觀察法為人類學的核心方法，故主旨 C。

questions.q: According to the professor, who pioneered participant observation?
options: A modern survey company
Bronislaw Malinowski, about a century ago
The people being studied
A group of grammar teachers
questions.why: 教授明說參與觀察由 Malinowski 約一世紀前開創，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention rules of grammar?
options: To show that people follow cultural rules they cannot easily articulate
To argue that language is unimportant
To explain how surveys work
To prove Malinowski was a linguist
questions.why: 以文法為喻，說明人們遵循自己說不清的規則，故文化多屬默會，故 A。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention the downsides at the end?
options: To conclude that the method is useless
To recommend using surveys instead
To praise the speed of the method
To give a balanced view of the method's limitations
questions.why: 結尾指出緩慢、主觀、觀察者改變情境等缺點，是對方法的平衡評價，故 D。

talk.text: Picture a merchant ship that sank in the Mediterranean three thousand years ago. Tragic for the sailors, but for archaeologists, a shipwreck is what we sometimes call a time capsule. Think about why. A settlement on land was occupied for centuries; its layers are mixed, things were added and removed over generations. But a shipwreck? Everything on that vessel went down together, on a single day. So the cargo, the sailors' personal items, the cooking pots, they all date to the same moment. That's an incredibly tight, sealed snapshot. And the sea can be a wonderful preserver. In cold, oxygen-poor water, or under a protective layer of silt, wood and rope and even food can survive for millennia, things that would have rotted away completely on land. The challenge, obviously, is getting to them. Underwater excavation is slow, expensive, and dangerous, and you can't just dig, you have to map everything with divers or robots before you disturb it. But when it works, a wreck can tell us exactly what people were trading, and how they lived, on one particular ancient voyage.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How ancient ships were built
The dangers faced by modern divers
Why the Mediterranean is dangerous
Why shipwrecks are valuable archaeological time capsules
questions.why: 全課說明沉船為何是珍貴的考古「時間膠囊」，故主旨 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor call a shipwreck a 'sealed snapshot'?
options: Because ships were sealed with wax
Because nothing valuable was ever aboard
Because everything on board dates to the same single day
Because the cargo was added over centuries
questions.why: 教授說船上一切在同一天沉沒，年代一致，故 C。

questions.q: What can be inferred about organic materials at underwater sites?
options: They always decay faster than on land
They can survive better than at many land sites
They cannot be recovered at all
They are never found on shipwrecks
questions.why: 教授說在低氧冷水或淤泥保護下，木、繩、食物可保存千年，反而優於陸地，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor contrast a shipwreck with a settlement on land?
options: To highlight that a wreck captures a single moment while a settlement is mixed over time
To argue that land sites are worthless
To explain how ships are constructed
To describe modern diving equipment
questions.why: 拿沉船與陸地聚落對比，凸顯沉船是單一瞬間、聚落則歷時混雜，故 A。

talk.text: Let's talk about what may be the single most important change in human history: the shift from foraging to farming, what we call the Neolithic Revolution, beginning around ten thousand years ago. For hundreds of thousands of years, humans lived by hunting and gathering, moving with the seasons. Then, in several parts of the world independently, people began to plant crops and keep animals. Now, we tend to assume this was pure progress, an obvious upgrade. But the evidence is more complicated. Skeletons from early farming communities are often shorter, and show more signs of disease and hard labor, than those of the foragers who came before. Their diet was less varied. So why did they do it? One likely answer is that farming, even if it made individuals less healthy, could feed far more people from the same patch of land. And more food meant bigger populations, permanent villages, and eventually cities, writing, and states. So farming didn't necessarily make people better off. It made them more numerous, and it set humanity on the road to everything we call civilization.

questions.q: What is the main point of the lecture?
options: The shift to farming had complex, mixed consequences for humanity
Foragers were unable to find enough food
Farming was invented in only one place
Ancient skeletons are hard to study
questions.why: 教授核心是農業轉型後果複雜、有得有失，故主旨 A。

questions.q: According to the professor, what do skeletons from early farming communities often show?
options: Greater height and better health
Shorter stature and more signs of disease
No difference from foragers
Evidence of much longer lifespans
questions.why: 教授說早期農民骨骼常較矮、疾病與勞損跡象更多，故 B。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why farming spread despite its downsides?
options: It made every individual healthier
It required less work than foraging
It reduced the human population
It could support many more people per unit of land
questions.why: 教授說農業能以同樣土地養活更多人，故雖不利個體健康仍擴散，故 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention cities and writing?
options: To argue that foragers had cities too
To explain how skeletons are dated
To show the long-term outcomes that farming made possible
To prove that farming improved individual health
questions.why: 提及城市與文字，是說明農業帶來的長遠結果，故 C。

talk.text: Here's a problem archaeologists faced long before radiocarbon dating existed: how do you put sites in chronological order when you have no way to get an absolute date? One clever solution is called seriation, and pottery is perfect for it. The insight is this: styles change over time, and they change in a predictable way. Think about cars, or clothes. A new style appears, it becomes popular, it peaks, and then it gradually fades as something newer takes over. Pottery decoration behaves the same way. So imagine you dig up trash pits at several sites, and in each one you count what percentage of the pottery is style A, style B, style C. A site where style A is ninety percent and style B is just appearing is probably early. A site where style A is gone and style C dominates is later. By arranging the sites so that each style rises and falls smoothly, you reconstruct the sequence. You still don't know the exact years, but you've put everything in the right order, and that's often the essential first step.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How pottery was manufactured
Seriation as a way to order sites chronologically
The invention of radiocarbon dating
Why people threw away pottery
questions.why: 全課介紹排序法（seriation）如何排出遺址先後，故主旨 B。

questions.q: According to the professor, how does a pottery style typically change over time?
options: It stays constant forever
It appears and disappears instantly
It appears, becomes popular, peaks, then fades
It becomes more popular every year without end
questions.why: 教授說風格會出現、流行、達峰、再消退，故 C。

questions.q: A site where style A is 90% and style B is just appearing is probably:
options: Relatively early in the sequence
The most recent site
Impossible to place in order
From a different region entirely
questions.why: A 佔九成、B 剛出現，依風格興衰，該遺址屬序列早期，故 A。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention cars and clothes?
options: To explain how pottery was traded
To argue that modern styles are better
To describe what was found in trash pits
To offer a familiar analogy for how styles rise and fall
questions.why: 以汽車、服飾比喻風格的興衰規律，幫助理解排序法，故 D。

talk.text: In the towns of medieval Europe, if you wanted to work as, say, a baker or a shoemaker or a goldsmith, you generally couldn't just open up shop. You had to belong to a guild, an association of craftsmen in the same trade. And guilds did a lot of things at once. First, they controlled quality. A guild set standards for its craft, and it could punish a member who sold shoddy goods, because one bad shoemaker damaged the reputation of all of them. Second, they controlled competition. The guild limited how many people could practice the trade and often fixed prices, which protected members from being undercut. Third, and this surprises people, they were social safety nets. If a member fell ill or died, the guild might support his widow and children. Now there's a career ladder built into this: you started as an apprentice, working for room and board while you learned, became a journeyman earning wages, and if you produced a 'masterpiece' judged good enough, you could become a master with your own shop. So the guild wasn't just an economic body. It shaped a craftsman's entire life.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Medieval shoes were poorly made
How to bake bread in medieval towns
The many roles guilds played in medieval craftsmen's lives
Why prices rose in medieval Europe
questions.why: 全課說明行會在工匠生活中扮演的多重角色，故主旨 C。

questions.q: According to the professor, why might a guild punish a member who sold shoddy goods?
options: Because the government required it
Because shoddy goods were illegal everywhere
Because the member had not paid his dues
Because one bad craftsman hurt the reputation of all members
questions.why: 教授說一個劣質鞋匠會損害全體聲譽，故行會會懲罰，故 D。

questions.q: What can be inferred about a journeyman?
options: He owned his own shop
He had advanced beyond apprentice but was not yet a master
He was not allowed to earn wages
He had been expelled from the guild
questions.why: 教授描述學徒→熟練工（journeyman，領薪）→師傅的階梯，故熟練工介於學徒與師傅之間，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention supporting a member's widow and children?
options: To illustrate the guild's role as a social safety net
To explain how prices were fixed
To describe the apprenticeship system
To show that guilds were purely economic
questions.why: 照顧遺孀孤兒是行會作為社會安全網角色的例證，故 A。

talk.text: For centuries, Egyptian hieroglyphs were a complete mystery. You could stand in front of a temple wall covered in these beautiful symbols and have no idea what they said. The knowledge of how to read them had simply died out. What broke the code was a single remarkable object: the Rosetta Stone, discovered in 1799. Now what made it so valuable? The stone carried the same message written three times, in three scripts: hieroglyphs, a later Egyptian script, and, crucially, ancient Greek, which scholars could still read. So it was a kind of built-in dictionary. If you knew what the Greek said, you had a huge clue to what the hieroglyphs must say. But it still took over twenty years, and the key insight came from a scholar named Champollion. Earlier researchers had assumed hieroglyphs were purely symbolic, each picture standing for an idea. Champollion realized that many of the signs actually represented sounds, like letters in an alphabet. Once he grasped that, the whole system began to unlock, and a lost language could speak again after nearly two thousand years of silence.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: The architecture of Egyptian temples
The complete life of Champollion
How the Rosetta Stone was discovered
How Egyptian hieroglyphs were finally deciphered
questions.why: 全課講埃及象形文字如何最終被破譯，故主旨 D。

questions.q: According to the professor, what made the Rosetta Stone so valuable?
options: It carried the same text in three scripts, including readable Greek
It was made of solid gold
It was the largest stone ever found
It was written entirely in hieroglyphs
questions.why: 教授說石碑以三種文字刻同一訊息，含可讀的希臘文，如同對照字典，故 A。

questions.q: What was Champollion's key insight?
options: Hieroglyphs were purely decorative
The Greek text was a forgery
Many hieroglyphic signs represented sounds, not just ideas
The stone was too damaged to read
questions.why: 教授說商博良領悟許多符號代表「聲音」而非純表意，故 C。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention earlier researchers' assumptions?
options: To praise their complete success
To contrast with Champollion's breakthrough
To explain how the stone was carved
To describe the Greek language
questions.why: 提早期學者「純表意」的錯誤假設，是為對比商博良的突破，故 B。

talk.text: Let's think about language as more than just a tool for communication. Because language is also one of the most powerful markers of identity that human beings have. Consider what happens when a community's language is threatened. Around the world, languages are disappearing at an alarming rate; linguists estimate that many of the roughly seven thousand languages spoken today will be gone within a century. And when a language dies, it's not only words that vanish. Each language carries a unique way of categorizing the world, of telling stories, of encoding what a community knows about its environment, its plants, its history. Some languages have rich vocabularies for concepts that others barely express. So losing a language can mean losing an entire body of knowledge. This is why many communities fight so hard to keep their languages alive, teaching them to children, creating written forms, recording elders. It's not mere nostalgia. For many people, to speak your ancestral language is to hold on to who you are. And that's why anthropologists treat language loss not as a minor footnote but as a genuine cultural emergency.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Language is a marker of identity, and its loss threatens cultural knowledge
There are exactly seven thousand languages
All languages will disappear within a century
Communication is the only purpose of language
questions.why: 教授核心是語言承載身分與知識，其消亡是文化危機，故主旨 A。

questions.q: According to the professor, what do linguists estimate about today's languages?
options: All of them are growing stronger
None of them will survive
They will all merge into one
Many of the roughly seven thousand may be gone within a century
questions.why: 教授引語言學家估計，約七千種語言中許多將於一世紀內消失，故 D。

questions.q: What can be inferred about why communities record their elders?
options: Elders enjoy being famous
Elders hold linguistic and cultural knowledge worth preserving
Written forms are useless
Young people cannot learn languages
questions.why: 錄下長者是為保存其承載的語言與文化知識，故 B。

questions.q: Why does the professor say language loss is not 'mere nostalgia'?
options: To argue that the past is unimportant
To recommend forgetting old languages
To stress that real knowledge and identity are at stake
To explain how languages are written down
questions.why: 教授藉此強調語言消亡涉及真實的知識與身分，非單純懷舊，故 C。

talk.text: One of the biggest challenges in archaeology is simply finding sites in the first place, especially when they're hidden under dense forest or buried below the surface. For a long time, discovery depended on luck, or on decades of walking the ground. But technology has transformed this. Let me tell you about LiDAR. LiDAR works by mounting a laser scanner on an aircraft and firing hundreds of thousands of pulses of light at the ground every second. Most of those pulses hit the treetops, sure, but a few slip through gaps in the canopy all the way to the forest floor. By filtering out everything the light bounced off above ground, you can build a detailed map of the bare earth, as if you'd stripped away all the vegetation. And the results have been stunning. In the jungles of Central America, LiDAR has revealed entire Maya cities, causeways, and field systems that had been swallowed by rainforest and missed by ground surveys for over a century. So a technology originally developed for other purposes has let archaeologists see through the trees, and it's rewriting what we thought we knew about how many people once lived there.

questions.q: What is the lecture mainly about?
options: How lasers were first invented
How LiDAR technology helps archaeologists find hidden sites
The daily life of the Maya
Why rainforests are disappearing
questions.why: 全課講 LiDAR 如何協助考古學家發現隱藏遺址，故主旨 B。

questions.q: According to the professor, how does LiDAR reveal the ground beneath a forest?
options: By filtering out pulses that bounced off vegetation to map the bare earth
By cutting down all the trees first
By digging test pits every few meters
By photographing the treetops in color
questions.why: 教授說濾除地面以上反射，重建裸地地形，如同剝除植被，故 A。

questions.q: What can be inferred about ground surveys in Central America before LiDAR?
options: They had already found all the Maya cities
They were never carried out
They missed features hidden by the rainforest
They were faster than LiDAR
questions.why: 教授說這些城市與道路被雨林吞沒、地面調查逾百年未發現，故推論地面調查漏掉了它們，故 C。

questions.q: Why does the professor note that LiDAR was 'originally developed for other purposes'?
options: To argue archaeologists should not use it
To explain how lasers damage sites
To criticize the cost of the technology
To highlight how a borrowed technology transformed the field
questions.why: 教授指出這項為他用途開發的技術意外革新了考古學，故 D。

talk.text: For our last topic, I want to correct a stereotype. When people hear 'nomads,' they often picture aimless wanderers, drifting around with no purpose. Nothing could be further from the truth. Nomadic pastoralists, people who make their living herding animals like sheep, goats, camels, or reindeer, move in highly organized, deliberate patterns. Here's the logic. In many environments, no single place has enough grass and water all year round. The mountains are lush in summer but buried in snow in winter. The lowlands are the reverse. So the smart strategy isn't to stay put and watch your herds starve; it's to move the animals to wherever the good pasture is, following the seasons along routes that families may have used for generations. This is a sophisticated adaptation to a harsh, variable landscape. And pastoralists rarely live in isolation. They typically trade their animal products, wool, milk, meat, with settled farmers for grain and manufactured goods. So rather than being outside civilization, nomads have usually been deeply connected to it, mobile specialists in a landscape where staying still would mean disaster.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the lecture?
options: Nomads wander aimlessly with no plan
Herding animals is easy work
Nomadic pastoralism is an organized, sophisticated adaptation
Farmers are superior to nomads
questions.why: 教授旨在糾正刻板印象，論證游牧是有組織、精巧的適應方式，故主旨 C。

questions.q: According to the professor, why do pastoralists move with the seasons?
options: Because they dislike their neighbors
Because no single place has enough grass and water all year
Because they are searching for lost animals
Because farmers force them to leave
questions.why: 教授說單一地點全年草水不足，故隨季節遷移至有牧草處，故 B。

questions.q: What can be inferred about the relationship between nomads and settled farmers?
options: They never had any contact
They were always at war
Nomads produced their own grain
They were economically interdependent through trade
questions.why: 教授說游牧民用畜產與農民換穀物與器物，可推論雙方經濟互賴，故 D。

questions.q: Why does the professor mention mountains and lowlands in different seasons?
options: To explain the logic behind seasonal migration
To argue that mountains are dangerous
To describe where farmers live
To show that nomads dislike snow
questions.why: 以山區與低地季節互補，說明季節性遷徙的合理邏輯，故 A。
